*HB1003.2*
January 27, 2026
HOUSE BILL No. 1003
_____
DIGEST OF HB 1003 (Updated January 27, 2026 11:17 am - DI 125)
Citations Affected: Numerous provisions throughout the Indiana
Code.
Synopsis: Boards and commissions. Makes changes to requirements
for the readoption of administrative rules. Repeals, merges,
consolidates, or otherwise modifies various boards, commissions, and
other governmental bodies. Modifies or establishes various funds.
Makes changes to the membership, duties, and operations of various
boards, commissions, and other governmental bodies. Provides that the
professional licensing agency may adopt and enforce procedural rules
for the administration of a board if the rule: (1) will affect multiple
boards; and (2) is not inconsistent with any rule adopted by the affected
board. Repeals the fire prevention and building safety commission
(commission). Transfers the commission's responsibilities and
administrative rules to the department of homeland security
(department). Makes certain changes to the administration of building
and safety statutes and building and safety codes. Provides for the
codification of administrative rules setting forth building and safety
codes into statute. Makes an appropriation.
Effective: Upon passage; May 15, 2026; July 1, 2026; January 1,
2027.
Bartels, Miller D, Pierce K,
Dant Chesser
January 8, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Government and Regulatory
Reform.
January 22, 2026, amended, reported — Do Pass. Referred to Committee on Ways and
Means pursuant to Rule 126.3.
January 27, 2026, amended, reported — Do Pass.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

January 27, 2026
Second Regular Session of the 124th General Assembly (2026)
PRINTING CODE. Amendments: Whenever an existing statute (or a section of the Indiana
Constitution) is being amended, the text of the existing provision will appear in this style type,
additions will appear in this style type, and deletions will appear in this style type.
Additions: Whenever a new statutory provision is being enacted (or a new constitutional
provision adopted), the text of the new provision will appear in this style type. Also, the
word NEW will appear in that style type in the introductory clause of each SECTION that adds
a new provision to the Indiana Code or the Indiana Constitution.
Conflict reconciliation: Text in a statute in this style type or this style type reconciles conflicts
between statutes enacted by the 2025 Regular Session of the General Assembly.
HOUSE BILL No. 1003
A BILL FOR AN ACT to amend the Indiana Code concerning state
and local administration and to make an appropriation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the State of Indiana:

37 Chapter 53.5. Government Reform Task Force
38 Sec. 1. As used in this chapter, "agency" has the meaning set
39 forth in IC 4-22-2-3.
40 Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, "council" refers to the legislative
41 council established by IC 2-5-1.1-1.
42 Sec. 3. As used in this chapter, "rule" has the meaning set forth
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

8
1 in IC 4-22-2-3.
2 Sec. 4. As used in this chapter, "task force" refers to the
3 government reform task force established by section 5 of this
4 chapter.
5 Sec. 5. The government reform task force is established.
6 Sec. 6. (a) Except as provided in subsections (b), (c), (d), and (e),
7 IC 2-5-1.2 applies to the task force.
8 (b) The task force consists of the following ten (10) members of
9 the general assembly:
10 (1) Three (3) members appointed by the president pro
11 tempore of the senate.
12 (2) Two (2) members appointed by the minority leader of the
13 senate.
14 (3) Three (3) members appointed by the speaker of the house
15 of representatives.
16 (4) Two (2) members appointed by the minority leader of the
17 house of representatives.
18 A member of the task force serves at the pleasure of the appointing
19 authority.
20 (c) The task force shall meet as called by the chair. All meetings
21 of the task force shall be open to the public in accordance with and
22 subject to IC 5-14-1.5. All records of the task force shall be subject
23 to the requirements of IC 5-14-3.
24 (d) A majority of the members of the task force constitutes a
25 quorum. The affirmative vote of at least a majority of the members
26 at a meeting at which a quorum is present is necessary for the task
27 force to take official action other than to meet and take testimony.
28 (e) The:
29 (1) chairman of the council shall designate the chair; and
30 (2) vice chairman of the council shall designate the vice chair;
31 of the task force from the members of the task force. The chair and
32 vice chair of the task force serve as chair and vice chair at the
33 pleasure of the appointing authority.
34 Sec. 7. The task force shall study:
35 (1) the feasibility of transferring the administration of the
36 office of the state chemist from Purdue University to the
37 Indiana state department of agriculture;
38 (2) any employment issue arising from a transfer of the
39 administration of the office of the state chemist; and
40 (3) any issue arising from a transfer of any property, records,
41 funds, or accounts of the office of state chemist to the Indiana
42 state department of agriculture.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

9
1 Sec. 8. The task force may request information or testimony
2 from department or agency heads, or the designee of a department
3 or agency head, as determined necessary by the task force.
4 Sec. 9. The task force shall:
5 (1) develop recommendations in a report concerning the
6 issues set forth in section 7 of this chapter; and
7 (2) submit the report to the council in an electronic format
8 under IC 5-14-6 not later than November 1, 2026.
9 Sec. 10. The legislative services agency shall provide support
10 staff to the task force.
11 Sec. 11. This chapter expires June 30, 2027.

13 SECTION 88, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
14 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 8. (a) There is created the affirmative action
15 advisory committee to assist in the effective implementation of the
16 affirmative action policy. The committee is composed of eight (8)
17 members. The governor shall appoint the members of the committee
18 with the advice of the affirmative action officer. The members serve at
19 the pleasure of the governor.
20 (b) A member of the committee is entitled to reimbursement for
21 traveling expenses and other expenses actually incurred in connection
22 with the member's duties, as provided in the state travel policies and
23 procedures established by the department of administration and
24 approved by the budget agency. A member who is not an officer or
25 employee of the state is entitled to the minimum salary per diem as
26 provided in IC 4-10-11-2.1(b) while performing the member's duties.
27 (c) The committee shall select from its membership a chairperson
28 and vice chairperson to serve for one (1) year from the date of
29 selection. They may be reelected at the pleasure of the committee. In
30 any instance where the chairperson or vice chairperson does not serve
31 the chairperson's or vice chairperson's full term, the committee shall
32 select another to serve in the chairperson's or vice chairperson's own
33 right a full term.
34 (d) The affirmative action advisory committee shall:
35 (1) provide liaison activities with the affirmative action officer
36 with respect to problems and suggestions concerning the
37 affirmative action policy;
38 (2) advise the affirmative action officer and the governor of
39 recommended changes in the implementation of the affirmative
40 action policy and improved guidelines for state agency programs;
41 and
42 (3) advise the governor and the affirmative action officer

1 concerning the effectiveness and status of the total
2 implementation of the affirmative action policy.
3 (e) The affirmative action advisory committee may review the

17 SECTION 11. IC 4-22-2.6-4, AS AMENDED BY P.L.213-2025,
18 SECTION 56, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
19 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 4. (a) To readopt a rule, an agency must conduct
20 a review of the rule to consider the continued need for the rule and
21 whether the rule, if readopted, will meet each of the standards in
22 IC 4-22-2-19.5 and (if applicable) the requirements for fees, fines, and
23 civil penalties in IC 4-22-2-19.6.
24 (b) In the review, the agency shall reexamine previous cost benefit,
25 economic impact, fiscal impact, and regulatory burden statements
26 prepared by the agency for the rule under IC 4-3-22-13, IC 4-22-2-22.7,
27 IC 4-22-2-22.8, IC 4-22-2-28, IC 4-22-2.1-5, or an executive order and
28 revise the statements to reflect any change in circumstances that affect
29 the analysis. The agency shall identify any alternative methods of
30 achieving the purpose of the rule that are less costly or less intrusive,
31 or that would otherwise minimize the economic impact of the proposed
32 rule on small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) and other
33 regulated entities. The agency also shall consider the following:
34 (1) The nature of any complaints or comments received from the
35 public, including small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4),
36 concerning the rule or the rule's implementation by the agency.
37 (2) The complexity of the rule, including any difficulties
38 encountered by:
39 (A) the agency in administering the rule; or
40 (B) small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) or other
41 regulated persons in complying with the rule.
42 (3) The degree to which technology, economic conditions, or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

13
1 other factors have changed in the area affected by the rule since
2 the last time the rule was reviewed.
3 (c) In the review, the agency shall compare the requirements
4 within the rule to similar requirements in Illinois, Kentucky,

6 of management and budget for comparison.
7 (c) (d) The agency shall prepare written findings concerning the
8 agency's determinations under this section. The written findings shall

20 (A) Any revisions to previously prepared cost benefit,
21 economic impact, fiscal impact, or regulatory burden
22 statements prepared by the agency for the rule under:
23 (i) IC 4-3-22-13;
24 (ii) IC 4-22-2-22.7;
25 (iii) IC 4-22-2-22.8;
26 (iv) IC 4-22-2-28; or
27 (v) IC 4-22-2.1-5;
28 if those previously prepared statements were published by
29 the Indiana Register.
30 (B) If the rule did not have a prior cost benefit, economic
31 impact, fiscal impact, or regulatory burden statement
32 prepared by the agency for the rule under:
33 (i) IC 4-3-22-13;
34 (ii) IC 4-22-2-22.7;
35 (iii) IC 4-22-2-22.8;
36 (iv) IC 4-22-2-28; or
37 (v) IC 4-22-2.1-5;
38 published in the Indiana Register, a copy of an updated
39 regulatory burden statement that meets the requirements
40 of IC 4-22-2-22.7.
41 (5) Any alternative methods of achieving the purpose of the
42 rule that are less costly or less intrusive, or that would
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

14
1 otherwise minimize the economic impact of the proposed rule
2 on small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) and other
3 regulated entities.
4 (6) The nature of any complaints or comments received from
5 the public, including small businesses (as defined in
6 IC 4-22-2.1-4), concerning the rule or the rule's
7 implementation by the agency.
8 (7) Any difficulties encountered by:
9 (A) the agency in administering the rule; or
10 (B) small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) or other
11 regulated persons in complying with the rule.
12 (8) The degree to which technology, economic conditions, or
13 other factors have changed in the area affected by the rule
14 since the last time the rule was adopted, readopted, or
15 amended.
16 (9) Whether the federal government or any of the states
17 covered by subsection (c) have less restrictive requirements
18 than the rule, and, if so, the evidence or unique circumstances
19 that justify why the more restrictive requirements in the rule
20 are necessary.
21 (10) The last time the substantive content of the rule was
22 amended.
23 (11) Whether the substantive content in the rule would be
24 more appropriately integrated into the Indiana Code as
25 opposed to remaining as a separate administrative rule. In
26 making such a determination, the agency shall consider the
27 frequency of updates to the rule since its initial promulgation.
28 If the substantive content of the rule has not been modified in
29 the prior eight (8) years, the agency must present specific
30 facts that justify keeping the substantive content in an
31 administrative rule rather than the Indiana Code.
32 (e) The written findings in subsection (d) must be submitted in
33 a form that can be easily loaded into commonly used business
34 analysis software and published in the Indiana Register using the
35 format jointly developed by the publisher, the office of
36 management and budget, and the budget agency. The office of
37 management and budget may provide more stringent requirements
38 for rules with fiscal impacts and costs above a threshold amount
39 determined by the office of management and budget.

35 libraries and library profession of Indiana, and to the citizens of the
36 state.

5 SECTION 8, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
6 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 39.1. (a) The state library advisory council is
7 established for the purpose of advising the board and the state librarian
8 concerning:
9 (1) general policies of the state library;
10 (2) plans or programs for library development and interlibrary
11 cooperation;
12 (3) library research;
13 (4) professional development for librarians;
14 (5) standards and rules for library services;
15 (6) administration and distribution of state and federal funds; and
16 (7) other matters as requested by the board and the state librarian.
17 (b) The advisory council consists of not more than fifteen (15)
18 members.
19 (c) The board shall appoint the members of the advisory council,
20 with nominations for appointment from library organizations and the
21 state librarian.
22 (d) Members of the advisory council shall serve two (2) year terms.
23 However, the board shall stagger the terms of the initial appointees.
24 (e) Notwithstanding subsection (d), if a member misses a majority
25 of the advisory council's meetings in a calendar year, the board may
26 remove the member and appoint a new member to serve the remainder
27 of the term of the member removed under this subsection.
28 (f) A member of the advisory council is not entitled to
29 compensation, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses.
30 (g) A quorum of the members must be present for the advisory
31 council to take any official action. A quorum of the advisory council
32 consists of a majority of the members appointed to the advisory
33 council. An affirmative vote by a majority of the members present is
34 needed for the advisory council to make a recommendation or take any
35 official action.
36 (h) This section expires December 31, 2026.

14 SECTION 39. IC 4-23-7.2-11 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE JULY
15 1, 2026]: Sec. 11. (a) The historical bureau shall establish the Indiana
16 historical marker program for marking historical sites in Indiana. As a
17 part of this program, the historical bureau shall fix a state format for
18 historical markers. No person may erect an historical marker in the
19 state format without the approval of the historical bureau. All historical
20 markers in the state format shall be provided by the historical bureau
21 using appropriated funds, local matching funds, donations, grants, or
22 any other funds provided for that purpose according to the guidelines
23 and rules of the historical marker program.
24 (b) The board may appoint a historical marker advisory committee
25 to serve without compensation. The committee may advise the board
26 and the director concerning the following:
27 (1) Guidelines and rules for the historical marker program.
28 (2) Appropriate sites to be marked.
29 (3) Other matters concerning the historical marker program as
30 requested by the board or the director.
31 (c) Historical markers approved under this section, including state
32 format markers installed after 1945 and markers installed by the
33 Indiana Civil War centennial commission, are the property of the state.
34 Maintenance of state historical markers is part of the historical marker
35 program. The historical bureau may cooperate with individuals, local
36 and state agencies, and private institutions and organizations for the
37 maintenance of the historical markers. Funds made available to the
38 historical marker program, as approved by the board, may be used for
39 necessary maintenance.
40 (d) No historical marker may be erected on a highway of the state
41 highway system without the approval of the historical bureau as to its
42 historical accuracy. This provision is in addition to any other
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

28
1 requirement of law.

5 SECTION 57. IC 4-23-35 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
6 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
7 JULY 1, 2026]:
8 Chapter 35. Indiana State Historical Records Advisory Board
9 Sec. 1. As used in this chapter, "board" refers to the Indiana
10 state historical records advisory board established by section 3 of
11 this chapter.
12 Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, "NHPRC" means the federal
13 National Historical Publications and Records Commission.
14 Sec. 3. The Indiana state historical records advisory board:
15 (1) initially established under Executive Order 06-01; and
16 (2) continued under Executive Order 13-13 and 17-06;
17 is established under the Indiana department of administration. The
18 board is a continuing board. The purpose of the board is to carry
19 out the duties under section 8 of this chapter.
20 Sec. 4. (a) The board consists of seven (7) voting members.
21 (b) At least four (4) members of the board must have recognized
22 experience in the administration of government records, historical
23 records, or archives.
24 (c) One (1) of the seven (7) members under subsection (a) must
25 be the state archivist. The remaining six (6) members shall be
26 appointed by the state archivist and are lay members who:
27 (1) may be reappointed; and
28 (2) serve at the will of the state archivist.
29 There is no term limit for members of the board.
30 (d) The state archivist shall serve as state coordinator and chair
31 of the board.
32 Sec. 5. (a) The term of a member appointed under section 4(a)
33 of this chapter is three (3) years.
34 (b) The state archivist shall appoint a new member to fill a
35 vacancy on the board that occurs for any reason. A member
36 appointed under this subsection serves the remainder of the
37 unexpired term of the vacating member.
38 Sec. 6. (a) This section applies to a member of the board
39 appointed under Executive Order 06-01, 13-13, or 17-06.
40 (b) An individual who serves as a member of the board on June
41 30, 2026, is appointed by operation of law to serve the remainder
42 of the member's unexpired term. A subsequent vacancy shall be
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

34
1 filled by the state archivist under section 4(c) of this chapter.
2 (c) This section expires July 1, 2030.
3 Sec. 7. (a) The board shall meet at least quarterly at the call of
4 the chair.
5 (b) The chair may call additional meetings. There is no
6 maximum number of meetings that may be called by the chair.
7 (c) A quorum consists of a simple majority of voting members.
8 Sec. 8. (a) The board shall serve as the central advisory body
9 for:
10 (1) historical records planning; and
11 (2) projects:
12 (A) funded by the NHPRC; and
13 (B) developed and carried out within Indiana.
14 (b) The board may do the following:
15 (1) Develop and submit to the NHPRC state priorities for
16 historical records programs as part of a state plan.
17 (2) Solicit or develop proposals for NHPRC grant projects.
18 (3) Review proposals by institutions in Indiana and make
19 recommendations about the proposals to the NHPRC.
20 (4) Work to preserve Indiana's documentary heritage.
21 (5) Promote practices that ensure preservation of, and access
22 to, the state's public and private records.
23 (6) Encourage adherence to archival and records management
24 principles through meetings and workshops.
25 (7) Survey repositories, support the state's records
26 management program, and promote the sharing of collection
27 information.
28 (8) Encourage the professional development of archivists,
29 curators, volunteers, and others dedicated to the task of
30 caring for Indiana's documentary heritage.
31 Sec. 9. (a) Each member of the board who is not a state
32 employee is not entitled to the minimum salary per diem provided
33 by IC 4-10-11-2.1(b). A member is, however, entitled to
34 reimbursement for expenses actually incurred in connection with
35 the member's duties as provided in the state policies and
36 procedures established by the Indiana department of
37 administration and approved by the budget agency.
38 (b) The expenses of the board shall be paid in the same manner
39 in which the board's expenses were paid under Executive Orders
40 06-01, 13-13, and 17-06.
41 (c) The Indiana department of administration shall staff the
42 board.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

35
1 Sec. 10. The affirmative votes of a majority of the members
2 appointed to the board are required for the board to take action on
3 any measure, including final reports.
4 Sec. 11. (a) The board shall annually submit a report on its work
5 to the governor.
6 (b) A report under subsection (a) is a public record.
7 SECTION 58. IC 4-23-36 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
8 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
9 JULY 1, 2026]:
10 Chapter 36. Indiana Cultural Commission
11 Sec. 1. As used in this chapter, "commission" refers to the
12 Indiana cultural commission established by section 3 of this
13 chapter.
14 Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, "Native American Indian" means
15 an individual who is at least one (1) of the following:
16 (1) An Alaska native as defined in 43 U.S.C. 1602(b).
17 (2) An Indian as defined in 25 U.S.C. 450b(d).
18 (3) A native Hawaiian as defined in 20 U.S.C. 7517(1).
19 Sec. 3. The Indiana cultural commission is established.
20 Sec. 4. (a) The commission consists of fifteen (15) members
21 appointed as follows:
22 (1) Two (2) members of the house of representatives
23 appointed by the speaker of the house of representatives. The
24 members appointed under this subdivision must be from
25 different political parties.
26 (2) Two (2) members of the senate appointed by the president
27 pro tempore of the senate. The members appointed under this
28 subdivision must be from different political parties.
29 (3) One (1) individual who is not a member of the general
30 assembly appointed by the speaker of the house of
31 representatives.
32 (4) One (1) individual who is not a member of the general
33 assembly appointed by the president pro tempore of the
34 senate.
35 (5) The secretary of the family and social services
36 administration, or the secretary's designee.
37 (6) The commissioner of the Indiana department of health, or
38 the commissioner's designee.
39 (7) The secretary of education, or the secretary's designee.
40 (8) The commissioner of the department of correction, or the
41 commissioner's designee.
42 (9) The director of the civil rights commission, or the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

36
1 director's designee.
2 (10) Four (4) individuals appointed by the governor in
3 accordance with subsection (b).
4 (b) The following apply to members of the commission
5 appointed by the governor under subsection (a)(10):
6 (1) The appointed individuals may not be members of the
7 general assembly or state employees.
8 (2) The appointed individuals must represent the following:
9 (A) African Americans.
10 (B) The Hispanic/Latino community.
11 (C) Women.
12 (D) Native American Indians.
13 (c) If a legislative member of the commission ceases to be a
14 member of the legislative chamber from which the member was
15 appointed, the member ceases to be a member of the commission.
16 (d) A member of the commission:
17 (1) serves at the pleasure of the member's appointing
18 authority; and
19 (2) may be removed at any time by the appointing authority.
20 (e) If a vacancy on the commission occurs, the appointing
21 authority who appointed the former member whose position has
22 become vacant shall appoint an individual to fill the vacancy. An
23 individual appointed to fill a vacancy serves on the commission for
24 the remainder of the unexpired term of the individual's
25 predecessor.
26 Sec. 5. (a) A member of the commission may be reappointed for
27 successive terms.
28 (b) The following members serve a two (2) year term that
29 expires June 30 of an odd-numbered year:
30 (1) A member of the general assembly appointed to the
31 commission under section 4 of this chapter.
32 (2) A member appointed to the commission under section
33 4(a)(3) or 4(a)(4) of this chapter.
34 (c) A member appointed to the commission under section
35 4(a)(10) of this chapter serves a term that expires December 31,
36 2028, and each fourth year thereafter.
37 Sec. 6. (a) The commission shall meet:
38 (1) at the call of the chairperson as necessary to fulfill its
39 duties under this chapter; and
40 (2) at least once quarterly in each calendar year.
41 (b) The chairperson shall designate the following subcommittees
42 to meet at least two (2) times each year:
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

37
1 (1) African American affairs.
2 (2) Hispanic/Latino affairs.
3 (3) Women and families.
4 (4) Native American Indian affairs.
5 Each subcommittee shall submit an annual report on its activities
6 to the full commission.
7 (c) The chairperson may:
8 (1) designate other subcommittees to meet between
9 commission meetings; and
10 (2) require a subcommittee to report on its activities to the full
11 commission.
12 (d) A subcommittee designated under this section is comprised
13 of the following:
14 (1) At least three (3) members of the commission appointed by
15 the chairperson.
16 (2) Not more than three (3) members of the public appointed
17 by the chairperson.
18 A member of public appointed to a subcommittee must be selected
19 based on the individual's background and experience related to the
20 subcommittee's designated purpose.
21 (e) A member of the public who serves on a subcommittee is not
22 entitled to a per diem or a mileage allowance under section 9 of this
23 chapter.
24 Sec. 7. (a) At the first official meeting of the commission each
25 year, the members shall elect:
26 (1) a member to serve as the chairperson of the commission;
27 and
28 (2) a member to serve as the vice chairperson of the
29 commission.
30 (b) A member elected to serve as chairperson or vice
31 chairperson of the commission shall serve for a term of one (1)
32 year. The term of office expires at the first commission meeting
33 held in the following year.
34 (c) A vacancy in the office of chairperson or vice chairperson
35 must be filled by a member of the commission selected by vote of
36 the remaining members of the commission.
37 Sec. 8. Eight (8) members of the commission constitute a
38 quorum for conducting commission business. The affirmative vote
39 of at least eight (8) members is required for the commission to take
40 official action on any measure. A member of the commission may
41 participate in a meeting by electronic means in accordance with
42 IC 5-14-1.5-3.6 as long as at least five (5) members of the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

38
1 commission are physically present at the meeting.
2 Sec. 9. (a) Each member of the commission who is not a state
3 employee is entitled to the minimum salary per diem provided by
4 IC 4-10-11-2.1(b). The member is also entitled to reimbursement
5 for mileage and traveling expenses as provided under IC 4-13-1-4,
6 and other expenses actually incurred in connection with the
7 member's duties as provided in the state policies and procedures
8 established by the Indiana department of administration and
9 approved by the budget agency.
10 (b) Each member of the commission who is a state employee is
11 entitled to reimbursement for traveling expenses as provided under
12 IC 4-13-1-4 and other expenses actually incurred in connection
13 with the member's duties as provided in the state policies and
14 procedures established by the Indiana department of
15 administration and approved by the budget agency.
16 (c) Each member of the commission who is a member of the
17 general assembly is entitled to receive the same per diem, mileage,
18 and travel allowances paid to members of the general assembly
19 serving on interim study committees established by the legislative
20 council. Per diem, mileage, and travel allowances paid under this
21 subsection shall be paid from appropriations made to the
22 legislative council or the legislative services agency.
23 (d) Expenses paid under subsections (a) and (b) shall be paid
24 from appropriations made to the civil rights commission.
25 Sec. 10. The commission shall do the following:
26 (1) Identify, study, and research issues affecting
27 underrepresented and minority groups, including the groups
28 described in section 6(b) of this chapter, in the following
29 areas:
30 (A) Health care.
31 (B) Economic and business development.
32 (C) Employment.
33 (D) Education.
34 (E) Social and family development.
35 (F) Housing.
36 (G) Civil rights.
37 (H) The justice system.
38 (I) Any other aspect of society identified by the
39 commission.
40 (2) Promote cooperation and understanding between
41 communities throughout Indiana.
42 (3) Assess the needs of underrepresented and minority groups
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

39
1 and identify recommendations to resolve those needs.
2 (4) Promote full participation of underrepresented and
3 minority groups in all aspects of society.
4 (5) Identify legal and social barriers to underrepresented and
5 minority groups.
6 (6) Identify measures that could facilitate easier access to
7 state and local government services by underrepresented and
8 minority groups.
9 (7) Make recommendations to appropriate governmental
10 agencies regarding issues of preservation and excavation of
11 Native American Indian historical and archeological sites,
12 including the reburial of remains of Native American Indians,
13 that are consistent with IC 14-21-1-25.5.
14 (8) Monitor legislation, policies, and other legal developments
15 in order to make recommendations to the general assembly
16 and the governor that support the commission's purposes.
17 (9) Gather, study, and disseminate information on
18 underrepresented and minority groups through publications,
19 public hearings, conferences, and other means.
20 (10) Before November 1 of each year, submit an annual report
21 on the commission's activities to:
22 (A) the governor; and
23 (B) the legislative council in an electronic format under
24 IC 5-14-6.
25 Sec. 11. The commission may study topics that are not described
26 in section 10 of this chapter as assigned by:
27 (1) statute;
28 (2) the governor;
29 (3) the legislative council; or
30 (4) the chairperson of the commission.
31 Sec. 12. The commission may do the following:
32 (1) Designate and appoint members to subcommittees in
33 accordance with section 6 of this chapter.
34 (2) Transact business and enter into contracts that support
35 the commission's purposes.
36 (3) Apply for, receive, and disburse gifts, contributions, and
37 grants of funds or in-kind services.
38 (4) Adopt, rescind, and amend bylaws to regulate the conduct
39 of the commission's business.
40 (5) Assign duties to the commission's officers under the
41 commission's bylaws.

20 money in the fund.
21 (d) The fund consists of gifts, contributions, and money donated
22 to the commission.
23 (e) The treasurer of state shall invest money in the fund not
24 needed to meet the obligations of the fund in the same manner as
25 other public funds may be invested. Interest on investments made
26 under this subsection accrues to the fund.
27 (f) Money in the fund at the end of a state fiscal year does not
28 revert to the state general fund.
29 (g) Money in the fund is continuously appropriated for the
30 purposes described in subsection (a).

34 SECTION 2, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
35 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 60. (a) As used in this section, "adequate welding
36 standards" means specifications, guidelines, tests, and other methods
37 used to ensure that all structural steel welds meet, at minimum, the
38 codes and standards for such welds established in the welding code and
39 under rules adopted by the fire prevention and building safety
40 commission under IC 22-13-2-2. department of homeland security.
41 (b) As used in this section, "certified welding inspector" means a
42 person who has been certified by the American Welding Society to
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

57
1 inspect structural steel welding projects and conduct welder
2 qualification tests.
3 (c) As used in this section, "structural steel welding" means
4 structural welds, weld repair, the structural system, and the welding of
5 all primary steel members of a structure in accordance with the welding
6 code. The term does not include welding that is required by the
7 American Society of Mechanical Engineers to have its own
8 certification.
9 (d) As used in this section, "welding code" refers to the American
10 Welding Society structural steel welding code D1.1, as in effect on July
11 1, 2023.
12 (e) A contractor whose workers are welding the structural steel on
13 a project shall ensure that all of the following occur:
14 (1) The workers performing the structural steel welding have been
15 tested by, and hold a valid certification from, a facility that, or an
16 individual who, has been accredited by the American Welding
17 Society to test and certify welders and welding inspectors.
18 (2) All structural steel welds performed for the project meet
19 adequate welding standards and follow the project's special
20 provisions.
21 (3) All structural steel welding inspections listed in the project's
22 special provisions are completed by a certified welding inspector.
23 (f) If the department reasonably suspects a contractor has violated
24 this section, the department shall require the contractor to remedy the
25 violation not later than thirty (30) days after the department notifies the
26 contractor of the violation. The notification to the contractor must be
27 signed by the commissioner and sent by a method that enables the
28 department to verify receipt of the notice by the contractor. During the
29 thirty (30) day period, the contractor may continue to work on the
30 project. If the contractor fails to remedy the violation within the thirty
31 (30) day period, the department shall find the contractor not
32 responsible and determine the length of time the contractor is
33 considered not responsible by the department.
34 (g) In making the determination of the length of time a contractor is
35 not responsible under subsection (f), the department shall consider the
36 severity of the violation. The period during which a contractor is
37 considered not responsible:
38 (1) may not exceed forty-eight (48) months; and
39 (2) begins on the date of substantial completion of the project.
40 (h) A finding by the department under subsection (f) that a
41 contractor is not responsible may not be used by another state agency
42 or a political subdivision in making a determination as to whether the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

58
1 contractor is responsible for purposes of that state agency's or political
2 subdivision's award of a public works contract to that contractor.

1 programs.
2 (9) Organization of manpower and chains of command.
3 (10) Coordination of federal, state, and local disaster activities.
4 (11) Coordination of the state disaster plan with the disaster plans
5 of the federal government.
6 (12) Other necessary matters.
7 (b) The agency shall take an integral part in the development and
8 revision of local and interjurisdictional disaster plans prepared under
9 section 17 of this chapter. The agency shall employ or otherwise secure
10 the services of professional and technical personnel capable of
11 providing expert assistance to political subdivisions, a political
12 subdivision's disaster agencies, and interjurisdictional planning and
13 disaster agencies. These personnel:
14 (1) shall consult with subdivisions and government agencies on
15 a regularly scheduled basis;
16 (2) shall make field examinations of the areas, circumstances, and
17 conditions to which particular local and interjurisdictional
18 disaster plans are intended to apply; and
19 (3) may suggest revisions.
20 (c) In preparing and revising the state disaster plan, the agency shall
21 seek the advice and assistance of local government, business, labor,
22 industry, agriculture, civic and volunteer organizations, and community
23 leaders. In advising local and interjurisdictional agencies, the agency
24 shall encourage local and interjurisdictional agencies to seek advice
25 from the sources specified in this subsection.
26 (d) The state disaster plan or any part of the plan may be
27 incorporated in rules of the agency or by executive orders.
28 (e) The agency shall do the following:
29 (1) Determine requirements of the state and political subdivisions
30 for food, clothing, and other necessities in the event of an
31 emergency.
32 (2) Procure and pre-position supplies, medicines, materials, and
33 equipment.
34 (3) Adopt standards and requirements for local and
35 interjurisdictional disaster plans.
36 (4) Provide for mobile support units.
37 (5) Assist political subdivisions, political subdivisions' disaster
38 agencies, and interjurisdictional disaster agencies to establish and
39 operate training programs and public information programs.
40 (6) Make surveys of industries, resources, and facilities in
41 Indiana, both public and private, necessary to carry out this
42 chapter.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

64
1 (7) Plan and make arrangements for the availability and use of
2 any private facilities, services, and property, and if necessary and
3 if the private facilities, services, or property is used, provide for
4 payment for the use under agreed upon terms and conditions.
5 (8) Establish a register of persons with types of training and skills
6 important in emergency prevention, preparedness, response, and
7 recovery.
8 (9) Establish a register of mobile and construction equipment and
9 temporary housing available for use in a disaster emergency.
10 (10) Prepare, for issuance by the governor, executive orders,
11 proclamations, and regulations necessary or appropriate in coping
12 with disaster.
13 (11) Cooperate with the federal government and any public or
14 private agency or entity in achieving any purpose of this chapter
15 and in implementing programs for disaster prevention,
16 preparation, response, and recovery.
17 (12) Do other things necessary, incidental, or appropriate to
18 implement this chapter.
19 (f) The agency shall ascertain the rapid and efficient
20 communications that exist in times of disaster emergencies. The agency
21 shall consider the desirability of supplementing these communications
22 resources or of integrating these resources into a comprehensive
23 intrastate or state-federal telecommunications or other communications
24 system or network. In studying the character and feasibility of any
25 system, the agency shall evaluate the possibility of multipurpose use of
26 the system for general state and local governmental purposes. The
27 agency shall make appropriate recommendations to the governor.
28 (g) The agency shall assist political subdivisions in implementing
29 the intrastate mutual aid compact created by section 10.8 of this
30 chapter.

8 SECTION 106. IC 10-18-1-3 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE
9 JANUARY 1, 2027]. Sec. 3. (a) The governor shall appoint members
10 of the commission for a term of three (3) years, subject to removal as
11 provided in this section.
12 (b) The commissioners:
13 (1) must be persons of high standing and character; and
14 (2) serve without compensation, except for reimbursement for any
15 reasonable expenses necessarily incurred by the commissioners
16 in the performance of their duties.
17 (c) The commissioners shall be selected without regard to their
18 political affiliations. However, not more than six (6) of the
19 commissioners at any time may be members of the same political party.
20 (d) The governor may, for just cause, based upon written charges
21 specifying alleged misconduct, remove any member of the commission,
22 after notice to the member and a public hearing.
23 (e) The governor shall appoint a qualified person to fill the
24 unexpired term of a member who does not complete the member's term.

37 the purposes of the fund.

39 the state general fund.

27 inviolate for the purpose originally designed.

42 member of the board.

86
1 (f) A vacancy shall be filled for the balance of an unexpired term in
2 the same manner as the original appointment.
3 (g) The treasurer of state shall serve as chairperson of the board.
4 The board shall annually elect one (1) of its ex officio members as vice
5 chairperson and may elect any other officer the board desires. The
6 board shall meet at the call of the chairperson and as provided in the
7 bylaws of the authority.
8 (h) The governor appointing authority may remove an appointed
9 member for misfeasance, malfeasance, willful neglect of duty, or other
10 cause.

12 minimum salary per diem provided by IC 4-10-11-2.1(b). However,
13 each appointed member is entitled to reimbursement for traveling
14 expenses and other expenses actually incurred in connection with the
15 member's duties.

20 serve as an ex officio member of the board in the absence of the ex
21 officio member.
22 (l) The majority of the members of the board constitute a quorum for
23 the purposes of conducting the board's business and exercising the
24 board's powers and for all other purposes. Vacant positions may not be
25 counted when determining whether a majority of the members is
26 present.
27 (m) The affirmative vote of a majority of all the members of the
28 board who are present is necessary for the authority to take action. A
29 vacancy in the membership of the board does not impair the right of a
30 quorum to exercise all the rights and perform all the duties of the
31 authority. An action taken by the board under this article may be
32 authorized by:
33 (1) resolution at any regular or special meeting; or
34 (2) unanimous consent of all the members who have not
35 abstained.
36 A resolution takes effect immediately upon adoption and need not be
37 published or posted.

34 (B) disbursing currency from an account;

36 (D) distributing assistance through an EBT system as
37 described in this chapter.
38 (i) (h) As used in this chapter, "primary business" means more than
39 fifty percent (50%) of the gross retail income (as defined in
40 IC 6-2.5-1-5) attributable to the location or premises where the
41 business is located.
42 (j) (i) As used in this chapter, "retailer" means a person that, in the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

1 ordinary course of business:
2 (1) sells or transfers tangible personal property; or
3 (2) provides or performs services for compensation;
4 to consumers.

6 the United States Department of Agriculture.

13 the form and manner specified by the division.
14 (2) Documents that compliance with an alternative method of
15 compliance approved by the division will not be adverse to the

17 applicant for the variance, as determined by the division.
18 (c) A variance granted under subsection (b) must be conditioned
19 upon compliance with the alternative method approved by the division.
20 Noncompliance constitutes the violation of a rule of the division and
21 may be the basis for revoking the variance.

23 following:
24 (1) Submits to the division a written request for the waiver in the
25 form and manner specified by the division.
26 (2) Documents that compliance with the rule specified in the
27 application for the waiver will create an undue hardship on the
28 applicant for the waiver, as determined by the division.
29 (3) Documents that the applicant for the waiver will be in
30 substantial compliance with the rules adopted by the division after
31 the waiver is granted, as determined by the division.

7 of the fire prevention and building safety commission
8 department of homeland security concerning fire drills.
9 For purposes of this subsection, occupant load is determined by
10 dividing the total square footage of the area used by the child care
11 ministry by thirty-five (35) and rounding any result that is not a whole
12 number up to the next whole number.
13 (d) The state fire marshal shall make an inspection of a child care
14 ministry registered under section 2 of this chapter at least annually.
15 (e) During an inspection, the state fire marshal shall inspect the
16 structure in which the child care ministry is conducted for fire safety
17 and life safety with respect to the structure's primary use.

16 diversion project for the following purposes:
17 (1) Determining the most practical and effective means of:
18 (A) addressing domestic supply chain disruptions that result

23 (iii) manufacturing delays, employment fluctuations, and
24 stagnant growth;

35 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 22.5. (a) The review board shall

40 approval of the review board. All historical markers in the state
41 format shall be provided by the review board using appropriated
42 funds, local matching funds, donations, grants, or any other funds
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

111
1 provided for that purpose according to the guidelines and rules of
2 the historical marker program.

4 committee to serve without compensation. The committee may
5 advise the review board and the director concerning the following:
6 (1) Guidelines and rules for the historical marker program.
7 (2) Appropriate sites to be marked.
8 (3) Other matters concerning the historical marker program
9 as requested by the review board or the director.

11 state format markers installed after 1945 and markers installed by
12 the Indiana Civil War centennial commission, are the property of
13 the state. Maintenance of state historical markers is part of the
14 historical marker program. The review board may cooperate with
15 individuals, local and state agencies, and private institutions and
16 organizations for the maintenance of the historical markers. Funds
17 made available to the historical marker program, as approved by
18 the review board, may be used for necessary maintenance.
19 (d) No historical marker may be erected on a highway of the
20 state highway system without the approval of the review board as
21 to its historical accuracy. This provision is in addition to any other
22 requirement of law.

29 (2) The executive and legislative body of each:
30 (A) county;
31 (B) municipality, if any; and
32 (C) conservancy district established under IC 14-33, if any;
33 in which the water sought in the request would be used.
34 (3) The executive and legislative body of each:
35 (A) county;
36 (B) municipality, if any; and
37 (C) conservancy district established under IC 14-33, if any;
38 in which the affected reservoir is located.
39 (d) Not later than seven (7) days after receiving a notice from the
40 department under subsection (c), each person described in subsection
41 (c)(1) shall, by certified mail, provide written notice of the request to
42 each:

1 (1) water utility; or
2 (2) other person;

7 adjust the annual cost of maintenance of the works of improvement as
8 stated in the plan, as calculated in subsection (d), if the following
9 conditions are met:

11 (A) the annual cost of maintenance of the works of
12 improvement as stated in the plan, and the year when the
13 annual cost was stated in the plan; and

15 of improvement.

17 either the state conservation engineer for the Natural Resources

26 and a copy of the annual report must be provided to the local circuit
27 court and the department of natural resources.
28 (d) The annual cost of maintenance of the works of improvement as
29 stated in the plan of a district may be adjusted to an amount not to
30 exceed the amount determined as follows:
31 STEP ONE: Determine the cost index from the cost index source
32 for the most recent year.
33 STEP TWO: Determine the cost index from the cost index source
34 for the year that the annual cost of maintenance of the works of
35 improvement was stated in the plan.
36 STEP THREE: Divide the number determined in STEP ONE by
37 the number determined in STEP TWO.
38 STEP FOUR: Multiply the result of STEP THREE by the annual
39 cost of maintenance of the works of improvement that is stated in
40 the plan.

32 SECTION 203. IC 14-34-10-2 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
33 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 2. (a) As used in
34 this section, "higher or better uses" means postmining land uses that
35 have a higher:
36 (1) economic value; or
37 (2) nonmonetary benefit;
38 to the landowner or the community than the premining land uses.
39 (b) In addition to other standards a permittee must meet under rules
40 of the commission, a permittee shall do the following:
41 (1) Place markers on the site to readily identify the permit area.
42 (2) Conduct the surface coal mining operation in a manner that
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

123
1 maximizes the use and conservation of the solid fuel resource that
2 is recovered so that reaffecting the land in the future through
3 surface coal mining is minimized.
4 (3) Restore the land affected to a condition capable of supporting
5 the uses that the land was capable of supporting before mining or
6 higher or better uses of which there is a reasonable likelihood if:
7 (A) those uses do not:
8 (i) present an actual or a probable hazard to public health or
9 safety; or
10 (ii) pose an actual or a probable threat of water diminution
11 or pollution; and
12 (B) the permit applicant's declared proposed land use
13 following reclamation:
14 (i) is not impractical or unreasonable;
15 (ii) is not inconsistent with applicable land use policies and
16 plans;
17 (iii) does not involve unreasonable delay in implementation;
18 or
19 (iv) does not violate federal, state, or local law.
20 (4) Except as provided in subdivisions (5) and (6) and section 4
21 of this chapter with respect to all surface coal mining operations
22 backfill, compact where advisable to ensure stability or prevent
23 the leaching of toxic materials, and grade to restore the
24 approximate original contour of the land with all highwalls, spoil
25 piles, and depressions eliminated. Small depressions are allowed
26 if needed to retain moisture to assist revegetation or as otherwise
27 authorized under this article.
28 (5) In a surface coal mining operation that:
29 (A) is carried out at the same location over a substantial time;
30 (B) transects the coal deposit and the thickness of the coal
31 deposit relative to the volume of the overburden that is large;
32 and
33 (C) has overburden and other spoil and waste materials at a
34 particular point in the permit area or otherwise available from
35 the entire permit area that is insufficient, giving due
36 consideration to volumetric expansion, to restore the
37 approximate original contour;
38 the operator, at a minimum, shall backfill, grade, and compact,
39 where advisable, using all available overburden and other spoil
40 and waste materials to attain the lowest practicable grade but not
41 more than the angle of repose to provide adequate drainage and
42 to cover all acid-forming and other toxic materials to achieve an
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

124
1 ecologically sound land use compatible with the surrounding
2 region.
3 (6) If in surface coal mining:
4 (A) the volume of overburden is large relative to the thickness
5 of the coal deposit; and
6 (B) the operator demonstrates that due to volumetric
7 expansion the amount of overburden and other spoil and waste
8 materials removed in the course of the mining operation is
9 more than sufficient to restore the approximate original
10 contour;
11 the operator shall, after restoring the approximate contour,
12 backfill, grade, and compact, where advisable, the excess
13 overburden and other spoil and waste materials to attain the
14 lowest grade but not more than the angle of repose and to cover
15 all acid-forming and other toxic materials to achieve an
16 ecologically sound land use compatible with the surrounding
17 region. The overburden or spoil shall be shaped and graded in a
18 way that prevents slides, erosion, and water pollution and
19 revegetated in accordance with the requirements of this article.
20 (7) Stabilize and protect all surface areas, including spoil piles,
21 affected by the surface coal mining and reclamation operation to
22 effectively control erosion and attendant air and water pollution.
23 (8) Remove the topsoil from the land in a separate layer and:
24 (A) replace the topsoil on the backfill area; or
25 (B) if the topsoil is not used immediately;
26 (i) segregate the topsoil in a separate pile from other spoil;
27 and
28 (ii) if the topsoil is not replaced on a backfill area within a
29 time short enough to avoid deterioration of the topsoil,
30 maintain a successful cover by quick growing plants or other
31 means so that the topsoil is preserved from wind and water
32 erosion, remains free of any contamination by other acid or
33 toxic material, and is in a usable condition for sustaining
34 vegetation when restored during reclamation.
35 However, if the topsoil is of insufficient quantity or of poor
36 quality to sustain vegetation or if other strata are more suitable for
37 vegetation requirements, the operator shall remove, segregate, and
38 preserve, in a like manner, the strata that are best able to support
39 vegetation.
40 (9) Restore the topsoil or the best available subsoil that is best
41 able to support vegetation.
42 (10) For all prime farmland as identified in IC 14-34-3-3(16),
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

125
1 comply with the specifications for soil removal, storage,
2 replacement, and reconstruction established by rules of the
3 commission and do the following:
4 (A) Segregate the A horizon of the natural soil unless it is
5 shown that other available soil materials will create a final soil
6 that has a greater productive capacity, stockpile this material,
7 if not used immediately, separately from other spoil, and
8 provide needed protection from wind and water erosion or
9 contamination by other acid or toxic material.
10 (B) Segregate the B horizon of the natural soil, or underlying
11 C horizons or other strata, or a combination of those horizons
12 or other strata that are texturally and chemically suitable for
13 plant growth and equal to or more favorable for plant growth
14 than the B horizon, in sufficient quantities to create in the
15 regraded final soil a root zone of comparable depth and quality
16 to that existing in the natural soil, stockpile this material, if not
17 used immediately, separately from other spoil, and provide
18 needed protection from wind and water erosion or
19 contamination by other acid or toxic material.
20 (C) Replace and regrade the root zone material described in
21 clause (B) with proper compaction and uniform depth over the
22 regraded spoil material.
23 (D) Redistribute and grade in a uniform manner the surface
24 soil horizon described in clause (A).
25 (11) Create, if authorized in the approved surface coal mining and
26 reclamation plan, permanent impoundments of water on mining
27 sites. The permittee may create the permanent impoundment only
28 after the permittee demonstrates the following:
29 (A) The size of the impoundment is adequate for the intended
30 purposes.
31 (B) The impoundment dam construction will be designed to
32 achieve necessary stability with an adequate margin of safety
33 compatible with that of structures constructed under 16 U.S.C.
34 1006.
35 (C) The quality of impounded water will be suitable, on a
36 permanent basis, for the intended use and discharges from the
37 impoundment will not degrade the water quality below water
38 quality standards established under applicable federal and
39 state law in the receiving stream.
40 (D) The level of water will be reasonably stable.
41 (E) Final grading will provide adequate safety and access for
42 proposed water users.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

126
1 (F) The water impoundments will not result in the diminution
2 of the quality or quantity of water used by adjacent or
3 surrounding landowners for agricultural, industrial,
4 recreational, or domestic uses.
5 (12) Conduct an augering operation associated with surface coal
6 mining in a manner that maximizes the recoverability of mineral
7 reserves remaining after the surface coal mining and reclamation
8 operation is complete and seal all auger holes with an impervious
9 and noncombustible material to prevent drainage except where
10 the director determines that the resulting impoundment of water
11 in those auger holes may create a hazard to the environment or the
12 public health or safety. The director may prohibit augering if
13 necessary to:
14 (A) maximize the use, recoverability, or conservation of the
15 solid fuel resources; or
16 (B) protect against adverse water quality impacts.
17 (13) Minimize disturbances to the prevailing hydrologic balance
18 at the mine site and associated offsite areas and to the quality and
19 quantity of water in surface and ground water systems during and
20 after surface coal mining and reclamation operations by doing the
21 following:
22 (A) Avoiding acid or other toxic mine drainage by measures
23 such as the following:
24 (i) Preventing or removing water from contact with
25 toxic-producing deposits.
26 (ii) Treating drainage to reduce toxic content that adversely
27 affects downstream water upon being released to
28 watercourses.
29 (iii) Casing, sealing, or otherwise managing boreholes,
30 shafts, and wells and keep acid or other toxic drainage from
31 entering ground and surface water.
32 (B) Conducting surface coal mining and reclamation
33 operations so as to prevent, to the extent possible using the
34 best technology currently available, violations of the effluent
35 limitations for coal mining operations established under
36 applicable state or federal law.
37 (C) Constructing siltation structures under clause (B) before
38 commencement of surface coal mining operations that will be
39 certified by an engineer licensed under IC 25-31 IC 25-4-3
40 and constructed as designed and approved in the reclamation
41 plan.
42 (D) Cleaning out and removing temporary or large settling
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

127
1 ponds or other siltation structures from drainageways after
2 disturbed areas are revegetated and stabilized and depositing
3 the silt and debris at a site and in a manner approved by the
4 director.
5 (E) Restoring recharge capacity of the mined area to
6 approximate premining conditions.
7 (F) Avoiding channel deepening or enlargement in operations
8 requiring the discharge of water from mines.
9 (G) Other actions required under the permit.
10 (14) With respect to surface disposal of mine wastes, tailings, coal
11 processing wastes, and other wastes in areas other than the mine
12 workings or excavations, the following:
13 (A) Stabilize all waste piles in designated areas through
14 construction in compacted layers, including the use of
15 incombustible and impervious materials if necessary.
16 (B) Assure the following:
17 (i) The final contour of the waste pile will be compatible
18 with natural surroundings.
19 (ii) The site will be stabilized and revegetated according to
20 this article.
21 (15) Refrain from surface coal mining within five hundred (500)
22 feet of active and abandoned underground mines to prevent
23 breakthroughs and to protect the health or safety of miners.
24 However, the director shall permit an operator to mine near,
25 through, or partially through an abandoned underground mine or
26 closer to an active underground mine if the following conditions
27 exist:
28 (A) The nature, timing, and sequencing of the approximate
29 coincidence of specific coal surface mining activities with
30 specific underground coal mining activities are jointly
31 approved by the regulatory authorities concerned with surface
32 coal mining regulation and the health and safety of
33 underground miners.
34 (B) The operations will result in:
35 (i) improved resource recovery;
36 (ii) abatement of water pollution; or
37 (iii) elimination of hazards to the health and safety of the
38 public.
39 (16) Design, locate, construct, operate, maintain, enlarge, modify,
40 and remove or abandon, in accordance with the standards and
41 criteria used by the United States Secretary of the Interior to
42 ensure that flood control structures are safe and effectively
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

128
1 perform their functions, all existing and new coal mine waste
2 piles:
3 (A) consisting of:
4 (i) mine wastes;
5 (ii) tailings;
6 (iii) coal processing wastes; or
7 (iv) other liquid and solid wastes; and
8 (B) used temporarily or permanently as dams or embankments.
9 (17) Ensure the following:
10 (A) All debris, acid-forming materials, toxic materials, or
11 materials constituting a fire hazard are treated, buried, and
12 compacted or otherwise disposed of in a manner designed to
13 prevent contamination of ground or surface water.
14 (B) Contingency plans are developed to prevent sustained
15 combustion.
16 (18) Ensure that explosives are used only in accordance with the
17 following:
18 (A) IC 14-34-12.
19 (B) Applicable state and federal law.
20 (C) The rules adopted by the commission.
21 (19) Ensure that all reclamation efforts proceed in an
22 environmentally sound manner and as contemporaneously as
23 practicable with the surface coal mining operations. However, if
24 the applicant proposes to combine surface coal mining operations
25 with underground coal mining operations to assure maximum
26 practical recovery of the mineral resources, the director may grant
27 a variance for specific areas within the reclamation plan from the
28 requirement that reclamation efforts proceed as
29 contemporaneously as practicable and permit underground coal
30 mining operations before reclamation if the following conditions
31 are met:
32 (A) The director finds in writing the following:
33 (i) The applicant has presented, as part of the permit
34 application, specific, feasible plans for the proposed
35 underground mining operations.
36 (ii) The proposed underground mining operations are
37 necessary or desirable to assure maximum practical recovery
38 of the mineral resource and will avoid multiple disturbance
39 of the surface.
40 (iii) The applicant has satisfactorily demonstrated that the
41 plan for the underground coal mining operations conforms
42 to the requirements for underground coal mining in that
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

129
1 jurisdiction and that permits necessary for the underground
2 coal mining operations have been issued by the appropriate
3 authority.
4 (iv) The applicant has shown the areas proposed for the
5 variance are necessary for the implementation of the
6 proposed underground coal mining operations.
7 (v) Substantial adverse environmental damage, either onsite
8 or offsite, will not result from the delay in completion of
9 reclamation as required by this article.
10 (vi) The provisions for the offsite storage of spoil will
11 comply with subdivision (25).
12 (B) The commission has adopted specific rules to govern the
13 granting of variances in accordance with this subdivision.
14 (C) Variances granted under this subdivision are to be
15 reviewed by the director not more than three (3) years from the
16 date of issuance of the permit.
17 (D) Liability under the bond filed by the applicant with the
18 director under IC 14-34-6 is for the duration of underground
19 coal mining operations and until the requirements of this
20 section and IC 14-34-6 are fully complied with.
21 (20) Ensure that the construction, maintenance, and postmining
22 conditions of access roads into and across the site of operations
23 will control or prevent the following:
24 (A) Erosion and siltation.
25 (B) Pollution of water.
26 (C) Damage to the following:
27 (i) Fish or wildlife or their habitat.
28 (ii) Public or private property.
29 (21) Refrain from the construction of roads or other access ways:
30 (A) up a stream bed or drainage channel; or
31 (B) in the proximity of a channel;
32 that seriously alters the normal flow of water.
33 (22) Establish on the regraded areas and all other land affected a
34 diverse, an effective, and a permanent vegetative cover:
35 (A) of the same seasonal variety native to the area of land to be
36 affected; and
37 (B) that is capable of self-regeneration and plant succession at
38 least equal in extent of cover to the natural vegetation of the
39 area.
40 However, an introduced species may be used in the revegetation
41 process where desirable and necessary to achieve the approved
42 postmining land use plan.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

130
1 (23) Assume the responsibility for successful revegetation, as
2 required by subdivision (22), as follows:
3 (A) On lands not eligible for remining, for five (5) full years
4 after the last year of augmented seeding, fertilizing, irrigation,
5 or other work to assure compliance with subdivision (22).
6 However, if the director approves a long term intensive
7 agricultural postmining land use, the applicable five (5) or ten
8 (10) year period of responsibility for revegetation commences
9 at the date of initial planting for the long term intensive
10 agricultural postmining land use. If the director issues a
11 written finding approving a long term intensive agricultural
12 postmining land use as part of the mining and reclamation
13 plan, the director may grant exception to subdivision (22).
14 (B) On lands eligible for remining, for two (2) full years after
15 the last year of augmented seeding, fertilizing, irrigation, or
16 other work in order to ensure compliance with subdivision
17 (22).
18 (24) Protect offsite areas from slides or damage occurring during
19 the surface coal mining and reclamation operations and not
20 deposit spoil material or locate any part of the operations or waste
21 accumulations outside the permit area.
22 (25) Place all excess spoil material resulting from coal surface
23 mining and reclamation activities to ensure the following:
24 (A) Spoil is transported and placed in a controlled manner in
25 a position for concurrent compaction and in a manner that
26 assures mass stability and prevents mass movement.
27 (B) The areas of disposal are within the bonded permit areas
28 and all organic matter is removed immediately before spoil
29 placement.
30 (C) Appropriate surface and internal drainage systems and
31 diversion ditches are used in a manner that prevents spoil
32 erosion and movement.
33 (D) The disposal area does not contain springs, natural
34 watercourses, or wet weather seeps unless lateral drains are
35 constructed from the wet areas to the main underdrains in a
36 manner that prevents filtration of the water into the spoil pile.
37 (E) If placed on a slope, the spoil is placed as follows:
38 (i) On the most moderate slope among the slopes on which,
39 in the judgment of the director, the spoil could be placed in
40 compliance with all the requirements of this article.
41 (ii) If possible, upon or above a natural terrace, bench, or
42 berm if the placement provides additional stability and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

131
1 prevents mass movement.
2 (F) Where the toe of the spoil rests on a downslope, a rock toe
3 buttress of sufficient size to prevent mass movement is
4 constructed.
5 (G) The final configuration is compatible with the natural
6 drainage pattern and surroundings and suitable for intended
7 uses.
8 (H) Design of the spoil disposal area is certified by an
9 engineer licensed under IC 25-31 IC 25-4-3 and in
10 conformance with professional standards.
11 (I) All other provisions of this article are met.
12 (26) To the extent possible using the best technology currently
13 available the following:
14 (A) Minimize disturbances and adverse impacts of the
15 operation on fish, wildlife, and related environmental values.
16 (B) Enhance those resources where practicable.
17 (27) Provide for an undisturbed natural barrier:
18 (A) beginning at the elevation of the lowest coal seam to be
19 mined; and
20 (B) extending from the outslope for a distance determined by
21 the director;
22 to serve as a barrier to slides and erosion.
23 (28) Replace the water supply of an owner of interest in real
24 property who obtains all or part of the owner's supply of water for
25 domestic, agricultural, industrial, or other legitimate use from an
26 underground or a surface source if the supply is affected by
27 contamination, diminution, or interruption proximately resulting

32 (29) Meet other criteria that are necessary to achieve reclamation
33 in accordance with the purposes of this article, taking into
34 consideration the physical, climatological, and other
35 characteristics of the site.

132
1 (2) the mining operation will remove an entire coal seam running
2 through the upper fraction of a ridge or hill, except as provided in
3 subsection (c)(1), by:
4 (A) removing all of the overburden; and
5 (B) creating a level plateau or a gently rolling contour;
6 (i) with no highwalls remaining; and
7 (ii) capable of supporting the postmining uses listed in
8 subdivision (1);
9 without regard to the requirement to restore the affected land to the

13 only if the following conditions are met:
14 (1) After consultation with the appropriate land use planning
15 agencies, if any, the proposed postmining land use is considered
16 an equal or a better economic or public use of the affected land
17 compared with the premining use.
18 (2) The applicant presents specific plans for the proposed
19 postmining land use and appropriate assurances that the use meets
20 the following conditions:
21 (A) The use is compatible with adjacent land uses.
22 (B) The use is obtainable according to data regarding expected
23 need and market.
24 (C) The use is assured of investment in necessary public
25 facilities.
26 (D) The use is supported by commitments from public
27 agencies where appropriate.
28 (E) The use is practicable with respect to private financial
29 capability for completion of the proposed use.
30 (F) The use is planned according to a schedule attached to the
31 reclamation plan so as to integrate the mining operation and
32 reclamation with the postmining land use.
33 (G) The use is designed by an engineer licensed under
34 IC 25-31 IC 25-4-3 and in conformance with professional
35 standards established to assure the stability, drainage, and
36 configuration necessary for the intended use of the site.

HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

133
1 review and comment on the proposed use.
2 (5) All other requirements of this article are met.
3 (c) With respect to a permit granted under subsection (a) and in
4 addition to other requirements the commission establishes by rule, the
5 director shall require the following:
6 (1) The toe of the lowest coal seam and the overburden associated
7 with the seam are retained in place as a barrier to slides and
8 erosion.
9 (2) The reclaimed area is stable.
10 (3) The resulting plateau or rolling contour drains inward from the
11 outslopes except at specified points.
12 (4) No damage is done to natural watercourses.
13 (5) Spoil is placed on the hilltop bench as is necessary to achieve
14 the planned postmining land use and the operator places all
15 excess spoil material not retained on the hilltop in accordance

17 (6) The operator ensures stability of the spoil retained on the
18 hilltop and meets all other requirements of this article.

31 as the successor agency.

34 other document is considered a reference to the commission, as the
35 successor entity.

37 pesticide review board is considered a rule of the commission.

16 Sec. 6. (a) The commission has the following duties:
17 (1) Carry out the commission's duties under IC 15-16-4.
18 (2) Recommend:
19 (A) priorities for projects;
20 (B) funding; and
21 (C) rules and laws;
22 concerning invasive species to the appropriate governmental
23 agencies and legislative committees.
24 (3) Recommend a lead state agency to:
25 (A) develop an invasive species inventory for each invasive
26 species taxon; and
27 (B) develop and maintain a data management system for
28 invasive species in Indiana.
29 (4) Communicate with other states, federal agencies, and state
30 and regional organizations to enhance consistency and
31 effectiveness in:
32 (A) preventing the spread of;
33 (B) early detection of;
34 (C) response to; and
35 (D) management of;
36 invasive species.
37 (5) Coordinate invasive species education and outreach
38 programs.
39 (6) Convene or support an invasive species meeting at least
40 once per biennium to provide information on best practices
41 and pertinent research findings.
42 (7) Assist governmental agencies in:
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

139
1 (A) reviewing current invasive species policies and
2 procedures; and
3 (B) addressing any deficiencies or inconsistencies
4 concerning invasive species policies and procedures.
5 (8) Assist state agencies in reviewing the agencies'
6 performance measures for accountability concerning the
7 agencies' invasive species actions.
8 (9) Receive reports from any governmental agency regarding
9 actions taken on recommendations of the commission.
10 (10) Apply for grants.
11 (11) Provide grants for education concerning or management
12 of invasive species.
13 (b) The commission does not have any regulatory authority over
14 invasive species or the authority to hear appeals of grievances.
15 (c) The commission may create advisory committees to provide
16 information and recommendations to the commission.
17 (d) The commission shall issue a report to the interim study

20 odd-numbered year. The report must include a summary of:
21 (1) the commission's activities;
22 (2) the performance of the commission's duties; and
23 (3) efforts in Indiana to identify and manage invasive species.
24 The report may include recommendations of the commission.
25 Sec. 7. (a) The commission on invasive species and pests'
26 invasive species council fund is established to carry out the
27 commission's responsibilities under section 6(a)(2) through 6(a)(11)
28 of this chapter. The fund shall be administered by the department.
29 (b) The fund consists of any of the following:
30 (1) Grants.
31 (2) Appropriations.
32 (3) Gifts and donations.
33 (c) The expenses of administering the fund shall be paid from
34 money in the fund.
35 (d) The state treasurer shall invest the money in the fund not
36 currently needed to meet the obligations of the fund in the same
37 manner as other public funds may be invested. Interest that
38 accrues from these investments must be deposited in the fund.
39 (e) Money in the fund may be used for purposes set forth in this
40 chapter and to meet the expenses of administering this chapter.
41 (f) Money in the fund at the end of a state fiscal year does not
42 revert to the state general fund.

141
1 (5) Provide counties and local communities conducting land use

12 SECTION 214. IC 15-12-5-7 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE
13 JANUARY 1, 2027]. Sec. 7. (a) The council consists of the following
14 members:
15 (1) The director or the director's designee.
16 (2) Nine (9) members appointed by the governor as follows:
17 (A) One (1) member representing county government.
18 (B) One (1) member representing municipal government.
19 (C) One (1) member representing farm owners.
20 (D) One (1) member representing home building and land
21 development.
22 (E) One (1) member representing business.
23 (F) One (1) member representing the environment.
24 (G) One (1) member with expertise in land use issues
25 representing academia.
26 (H) One (1) member representing soil and water conservation
27 districts.
28 (I) One (1) member representing forestry.
29 (b) Not more than five (5) of the members appointed by the
30 governor under subsection (a)(2) may be of the same political party.
31 (c) The term of a member is four (4) years.
32 (d) Each member appointed by the governor under subsection (a)(2)
33 is entitled to hold office for the term of the member's appointment and
34 is entitled to continue to serve after expiration of the member's
35 appointment until a successor is appointed and qualified.
36 (e) A member is eligible for reappointment.
37 (f) A member appointed by the governor under subsection (a)(2)
38 may be removed from office by the governor and serves at the pleasure
39 of the governor.
40 (g) If a vacancy occurs among the members of the council appointed
41 by the governor under subsection (a)(2), the governor shall appoint an
42 individual to serve for the unexpired term of the vacating member.

13 SECTION 233. IC 15-16-4-37, AS AMENDED BY P.L.249-2023,
14 SECTION 73, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
15 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 37. As used in this chapter, "restricted use
16 pesticide" means the following:
17 (1) Any pesticide classified as a restricted use pesticide by the
18 administrator of the United States Environmental Protection
19 Agency.
20 (2) A pesticide designated as a restricted use pesticide in a law
21 enacted by the general assembly.
22 (3) Subject to section 50 of this chapter, a pesticide that the board
23 commission has determined to be unduly hazardous to persons,
24 animals, plants, wildlife, waters, or lands other than the pests it is
25 intended to prevent, destroy, control, or mitigate.
26 (4) All formulations containing methomyl (Chemical Abstracts
27 Service Reg. No. 16752-77-5).
28 (5) Any dicamba containing pesticide product that:
29 (A) contains a dicamba active ingredient concentration greater
30 than or equal to six and one-half percent (6.5%); and
31 (B) is intended for agricultural production uses but is not
32 labeled solely for use on turf or other nonagricultural use sites.

36 chemist's finding of facts that are supported by the substantial evidence

8 the person's registration under this chapter.
