10 only in accordance with subsections (c) through (e). However, this
11 section does not require the state chemist to impose a civil penalty on
12 a person for any violation of this chapter or a rule adopted under this
13 chapter.
14 (c) Except for a violation described in subsection (d) or (e), the state
15 chemist may impose a civil penalty not exceeding two hundred fifty
16 dollars ($250) for a violation of this chapter or any rule adopted under
17 this chapter. A civil penalty imposed under this subsection may be
18 adjusted for mitigating actions described in subsection (f). A civil
19 penalty under this subsection may not be imposed unless a warning for
20 a violation has previously been issued to the person for the same
21 violation within the previous five (5) years.
22 (d) Subject to adjustment under subsection (f) for mitigating actions,
23 the state chemist may impose a civil penalty not exceeding five
24 hundred dollars ($500) if the state chemist finds that a person has done
25 any of the following:
26 (1) Unknowingly committed an act described in section 57(1) of
27 this chapter.
28 (2) Committed an act described in section 57(2) through 57(5) of
29 this chapter.
30 (3) Unknowingly committed an act described in section 66(1) of
31 this chapter.
32 (4) Committed an act described in section 66(2) or 66(3) of this
33 chapter.
34 (5) Stored or dispensed a general use pesticide in a manner
35 inconsistent with 40 CFR 165.
36 (e) The state chemist may impose a civil penalty of one thousand
37 dollars ($1,000) if the state chemist finds that a person has done any of
38 the following:
39 (1) Knowingly committed an act described in section 57(1) of this
40 chapter.
41 (2) Committed an act described in section 57(8) of this chapter.
42 (3) Knowingly committed an act described in section 66(1) of this
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

153
1 chapter.
2 (4) Committed an act described in section 67, 68, 73(c), or 77 of
3 this chapter.
4 (5) Stored or dispensed a restricted use pesticide in a manner
5 inconsistent with 40 CFR 165.
6 (6) After notice, refused to comply with this chapter, the rules
7 adopted under this chapter, or a lawful order of the state chemist
8 or board. commission.
9 (f) The state chemist may not adjust the amount of the civil penalty
10 for a violation listed in subsection (e). The state chemist may reduce
11 the amount of the civil penalty for a violation listed in subsection (c) or
12 (d) if the state chemist finds that the person responsible for the
13 violation has taken any of the following mitigating actions:
14 (1) Cooperated with the state chemist during the investigation or
15 inspection process.
16 (2) Took corrective action to prevent future similar violations.
17 (3) Compensated a victim for any adverse effects that resulted
18 from the violation.
19 The state chemist may reduce the amount of the civil penalty by twenty
20 percent (20%) for each mitigating action taken by the person
21 responsible for the violation.
22 (g) A proceeding under IC 4-21.5-3 that involves the imposition of
23 a civil penalty may be consolidated with any other proceeding
24 commenced under IC 4-21.5 to enforce this chapter or the rules
25 adopted under this chapter.
26 (h) The state chemist shall limit the imposition of a civil penalty in
27 a proceeding involving multiple counts of repeated incidents of the
28 same violation to five (5) counts.
29 (i) Any findings related to a violation of this chapter must be
30 disclosed to the alleged violator before a:
31 (1) final determination is made; and
32 (2) penalty is assessed.
33 (j) Unless the state chemist has evidence of wrongdoing by the
34 licensed pesticide business, any warnings, citations, or civil penalties
35 regarding a pesticide use in violation of this chapter must be directed
36 at:
37 (1) the employee who committed the violation; or
38 (2) the person who was responsible for the supervision of the
39 employee who committed the violation.
40 (k) Money collected for civil penalties imposed under this section
41 shall be credited to the office of Purdue pesticide programs. The money
42 may be used only to provide education about pesticides.

HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

155
1 SECTION 53, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
2 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 64. (a) A person may not produce, transport, store,
3 handle, or dispose of any pesticide product or pesticide product
4 containers in a manner that may:
5 (1) cause injury to:
6 (A) humans;
7 (B) beneficial vegetation;
8 (C) crops;
9 (D) livestock;
10 (E) wildlife; or
11 (F) beneficial insects; or
12 (2) pollute any waterway in a way harmful to any wildlife in a
13 waterway.
14 (b) The board commission may adopt rules governing the
15 production, transportation, storage, handling, and disposal of pesticide
16 products or pesticide containers. In determining these standards, the
17 board commission shall take into consideration any regulations issued
18 by the United States Environmental Protection Agency.
19 (c) Except for pesticides labeled for use in the treatment of drinking
20 water, pesticide storage, mixing, and loading is prohibited within the
21 isolation area of a wellhead protection area zone. Application of
22 pesticides within the isolation area is permitted unless prohibited by the
23 pesticide label.
24 (d) A pesticide storage container located within the wellhead
25 protection area zone and outside of the isolation area must be stored:
26 (1) on an impervious surface designed to contain a discharge of
27 the entire contents of the largest storage container plus the volume
28 displaced by all the other items within the contained area; and
29 (2) in an area protected from precipitation.
30 (e) A pesticide spill or discharge within the wellhead protection area
31 zone must be cleaned up immediately upon discovery and in a manner
32 that prevents the pesticide from threatening waters of the state, dry
33 wells, storm sewers, sanitary sewers, or septic systems.
34 (f) A person may not open burn a pesticide container in a manner
35 that allows air contaminants resulting from combustion to be emitted
36 directly into the air without passing through a stack or chimney from
37 an enclosed chamber, as allowed under 326 IAC 4-1.

1 (d) Whenever a hearing is held under this section, the board

9 SECTION 265. IC 15-18-2-14, AS ADDED BY P.L.2-2008,
10 SECTION 9, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
11 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 14. (a) Milk and cream must be purchased
12 on the basis of weight or volume and:
13 (1) the butterfat content;
14 (2) the protein content; or
15 (3) a combination of butterfat and protein content;
16 as determined by any test or tests approved by the board, which may
17 also have been designated official by AOAC International.
18 (b) A payment for milk must be made either on the basis of:
19 (1) a fresh sample taken and tested according to procedures
20 established under rules adopted by the board; or
21 (2) a composite test.
22 A composite sample must cover a period of not more than sixteen (16)
23 days. In each plant, all composite samples must cover the same period
24 for each test and succeeding periods must be as nearly equal as
25 possible. All composite samples must cover the same period. A
26 uniform sample consisting of at least ten (10) cubic centimeters of milk
27 must be taken each day, except when milk is frozen or partly churned,
28 from the milk furnished by each person. An approved preservative
29 must be added to the sample and each composite sample must be held
30 in an approved, rubber stoppered, airtight bottle that is plainly labeled
31 to show the name or number of the person whose milk the composite
32 sample represents. The milk to be sampled must be poured into a weigh
33 tank approved by the creamery license division board meeting the
34 standards prescribed in rules, be run through a fine mesh or perforated
35 metal strainer, and be homogeneous in richness before any sample is
36 taken. The composite samples must be kept in a refrigerated cabinet or
37 cooler in or near the receiving room at a temperature of not more than
38 forty-two (42) degrees Fahrenheit, away from strong light, and must be
39 kept free of mold. The test must be made within seventy-two (72)
40 hours, excluding Sundays and holidays, after the last sample has been
41 added to the composite sample.
42 (c) If at the close of any composite period the composite samples are
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

164
1 inaccurate as determined by the creamery license division, board, three
2 (3) daily samples must be taken within the first five (5) days of the
3 following composite period. The average of the three (3) fresh samples
4 determines the basis of payment for the composite period in question.
5 (d) In each plant where composite samples of milk are taken, the
6 owner or manager shall delegate an individual who is responsible for
7 the care, condition, and accuracy of all composite samples.
8 (e) After the composite samples are emptied, the sample bottles
9 must be thoroughly washed, and all bottles and rubber stoppers shall
10 be sterilized before being used again.
11 (f) Composite samples may be removed from the plant for testing
12 elsewhere only on written permission from the creamery license
13 division. board.

17 station, a milk shipping station, a milk factory, a milk distributor, a
18 cheese factory, a condensery, an ice cream factory, other processing
19 plant, person, or person's agent that handles, receives, buys, or pays for
20 milk or cream from a producer, a bargaining agent, or another plant
21 shall obtain a license from the creamery license division. board.
22 (b) A license must be issued to the dairy, dairy processing plant,
23 receiving station, milk factory, milk distributor, cheese factory,
24 condensery, ice cream factory, other processing plant, person, or
25 person's agent by the creamery license division board upon:
26 (1) compliance with sections 14 through 17 of this chapter; and
27 (2) payment of a license fee as provided in sections 22 and 23 of
28 this chapter.
29 A license shall be revoked by Purdue University upon recommendation
30 of the board if the licensee fails to comply with the rules under which
31 the license was granted.

8 processes, or equipment that are not described in the administrative
9 rules of the state department or the executive board concerning
10 residential onsite sewage systems (410 IAC 6-8.3) or commercial
11 onsite sewage systems (410 IAC 6-10.1).

13 review panel consisting of individuals with technical or scientific
14 knowledge relating to onsite sewage systems. The technical review
15 panel:
16 (1) shall decide under subsection (f) whether to approve:
17 (A) proprietary residential wastewater treatment devices; and
18 (B) proprietary commercial wastewater treatment devices;
19 for general use in Indiana;
20 (2) shall biannually review the performance of residential onsite
21 sewage systems and commercial onsite sewage systems;
22 (3) shall assist the state department in developing standards and
23 guidelines for proprietary residential wastewater treatment
24 devices and proprietary commercial wastewater treatment
25 devices;
26 (4) shall assist the executive board and the state department in
27 updating rules adopted under section 4 of this chapter concerning
28 residential onsite sewage systems and commercial onsite sewage
29 systems; and
30 (5) may exercise the powers granted by section 27.7 of this
31 chapter.

33 (1) A member of the staff of the state department, who shall serve
34 as the chair.

1 Scientists.

6 Professionals Association.
7 (8) An Indiana onsite sewage system contractor appointed by the
8 governor.
9 (9) A representative of the Indiana State Building and
10 Construction Trades Council.
11 All members of the technical review panel are voting members, except
12 the member described in subdivision (1).
13 (d) In the case of a tie vote of the technical review panel, the
14 following shall occur:
15 (1) If the tie vote is in regard to a county, city, or town ordinance
16 concerning residential onsite sewage systems:
17 (A) the technical review panel shall send the ordinance back
18 to the county, city, or town not more than seven (7) days after
19 the day of the tie vote;
20 (B) the county, city, or town may revise and resubmit the
21 ordinance; and
22 (C) if the ordinance is revised and resubmitted, the technical
23 review panel shall consider the revised ordinance at its next
24 meeting.
25 (2) If the tie vote is in regard to a matter not described in
26 subdivision (1), the technical review panel shall, not more than
27 seven (7) days after the day of the tie vote:
28 (A) contact the applicant by phone call and by mail; and
29 (B) request more information or provide an explanation of how
30 the applicant can modify the application to make it more
31 complete.
32 The technical review panel shall review any new information
33 provided by the applicant and vote again on the application not
34 more than thirty (30) days after receiving the information.

38 (A) residential onsite sewage systems under sections 4 and 27
39 of this chapter and IC 16-41-25; and
40 (B) commercial onsite sewage systems under sections 4 and 27
41 of this chapter and IC 16-19-3.5.
42 (2) Meet monthly as needed to assist the executive board and the

1 state department as described in subsection (b)(4) and to review

13 SECTION 287. IC 16-19-3-27.6, AS ADDED BY P.L.167-2022,
14 SECTION 5, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
15 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 27.6. (a) As used in this section, "type of
16 residential onsite sewage system" means an onsite sewage system that:
17 (1) has a particular design;
18 (2) consists of particular components; and
19 (3) is installed or constructed in a particular way.
20 (b) For purposes of this section, the following are different types of
21 residential onsite sewage systems:
22 (1) A conventional septic system consisting of a septic tank and
23 a gravel or stone soil absorption field.
24 (2) A chamber system in which wastewater is piped from the
25 septic tank to a series of connected chambers backfilled with soil.
26 (3) A subsurface drip distribution system in which wastewater is
27 pumped from the septic tank to a dose tank and then gradually
28 delivered to drip laterals in the soil.
29 (4) An aerobic treatment unit in which oxygen is injected into the
30 wastewater to accelerate the biological processes used to treat the
31 wastewater before being discharged to a soil absorption field.
32 (5) A recirculating sand filter system in which wastewater flows
33 from a septic tank to a pump chamber and then is recirculated
34 through a sand filter before being discharged to a soil absorption
35 field.
36 (6) An evapotranspiration system that includes a drain field
37 having a watertight liner and in which wastewater, after entering
38 the drain field, evaporates into the air.
39 (7) A constructed wetland system in which wastewater from a
40 septic tank enters a wetland cell containing an impermeable liner,
41 gravel, sand fill, and wetland plants that remove pathogens and
42 nutrients from the wastewater before being discharged to a soil
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

177
1 absorption field.
2 (8) A sand-lined system in which wastewater flows from a septic
3 tank to a combined treatment and dispersal soil absorption field
4 containing piping laterals surrounded by sand.
5 (c) If:
6 (1) the local health department of a county, city, or multiple
7 county unit has issued a permit for construction or installation of
8 a type of residential onsite sewage system;
9 (2) the use of the same type of residential onsite sewage system
10 at a location in a different county, city, or multiple county unit has
11 been approved by:
12 (A) a professional engineer registered under IC 25-31-1;
13 IC 25-4-3;
14 (B) a registered soil scientist (as defined in IC 25-31.5-1-7);
15 (C) an individual or entity engaged in the business of
16 constructing and installing residential onsite sewage systems;
17 and
18 (D) the designer of the system, if the system was designed by
19 someone other than a person referred to in clause (A) or (C);
20 and
21 (3) all of the persons referred to in subdivision (2) attest that the
22 same type of residential onsite sewage system, if constructed or
23 installed at the location referred to in subdivision (2), would
24 satisfy the requirements of the rule of the state department
25 concerning residential onsite sewage systems;
26 the local health department of the county, city, or multiple county unit
27 referred to in subdivision (2) may not refuse to issue a permit for the
28 construction or installation of a residential onsite sewage system of the
29 type referred to in subdivisions (1), (2), and (3) at the location referred
30 to in subdivision (2).

11 SECTION 303. IC 16-41-25-11, AS ADDED BY P.L.232-2023,
12 SECTION 13, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
13 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 11. (a) As used in this section, "residential
14 onsite sewage system failure" means a residential onsite sewage system
15 that exhibits one (1) or more of the following:
16 (1) The onsite sewage system refuses to accept sewage at the rate
17 of design application and interferes with the normal use of
18 residential plumbing fixtures.
19 (2) Effluent discharge exceeds the absorptive capacity of the soil,
20 resulting in ponding, seepage, or other discharge of the effluent
21 to the ground surface or to surface waters.
22 (3) Effluent is discharged from the onsite sewage system causing
23 contamination of a potable water supply, ground water, or surface
24 waters.
25 (b) A local health department that determines that a residential
26 onsite sewage system is in residential onsite sewage system failure may
27 issue an order requiring that the failure be corrected. However, an order
28 issued under this subsection is stayed if the owner or occupant of the
29 residence served by the residential onsite sewage system, within
30 fourteen (14) days after the date of the order, enters into a contract
31 with:
32 (1) a septic system installer or inspector registered with the
33 county in which the residential onsite sewage system is located;
34 (2) an onsite sewage system technician; or
35 (3) an Indiana professional engineer registered under IC 25-31-1;
36 IC 25-4-3;
37 under which the installer or inspector, onsite sewage system technician,
38 or professional engineer agrees to conduct an inspection to determine
39 whether the residential onsite sewage system is in residential onsite
40 sewage system failure.
41 (c) An order is stayed under subsection (b) until the earlier of the
42 following:
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

190
1 (1) The date on which the installer or inspector, technician, or
2 engineer performs the inspection and presents the results of the
3 inspection in writing to:
4 (A) the owner or occupant of the residence served by the
5 residential onsite sewage system; and
6 (B) the local health department.
7 (2) The expiration of forty-five (45) days after the date of the
8 order issued under subsection (b).
9 (d) If the installer or inspector, onsite sewage system technician, or
10 professional engineer states in the report provided to the local health
11 department that the residential onsite sewage system is not in
12 residential onsite sewage system failure, the local health department
13 shall:
14 (1) withdraw the order issued under subsection (b); or
15 (2) conduct an investigation of the residential onsite sewage
16 system to make a new determination whether the residential
17 onsite sewage system is in residential onsite sewage system
18 failure.
19 (e) If the local health department conducts an investigation under
20 subsection (d)(2), the order issued under subsection (b) is stayed
21 pending the new determination of the local health department based on
22 the investigation.

33 following the due date;
34 (2) the report is false; or
35 (3) the requirements of this chapter have not been complied with;
36 the state egg board may revoke the permit. If the fee is unpaid after the
37 ten (10) day grace period, a penalty of the greater of twenty dollars
38 ($20) or ten percent (10%) of the amount due in addition to the amount
39 due shall be assessed. If the state egg board determines that an account
40 review is necessary, out-of-state permit holders shall reimburse the
41 state egg board for expenses incurred to conduct the account review.

2 SECTION 345. IC 20-20-40-13, AS AMENDED BY P.L.162-2024,
3 SECTION 5, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
4 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 13. (a) The commission council has the
5 following duties:
6 (1) To adopt rules concerning the following:
7 (A) The use of restraint and seclusion in a school corporation
8 or a state accredited nonpublic school, with an emphasis on
9 eliminating or minimizing the use of restraint and seclusion.
10 (B) The prevention of the use of types of restraint or seclusion
11 that may harm a student, a school employee, a school volunteer,
12 or the educational environment of the school.
13 (C) Requirements for notifying parents.
14 (D) Training regarding the use of restraint and seclusion,
15 including the frequency of training and what employees must
16 be trained.
17 (E) The distribution of the seclusion and restraint policy to
18 parents and the public.
19 (F) Requirements for the reporting of incidents of restraint and
20 seclusion in the annual school performance report, including
21 incidents of restraint and seclusion involving school resource
22 officers (as defined in IC 20-26-18.2-1).
23 (G) Circumstances that may require more timely incident
24 reporting and the requirements for such reporting.
25 (2) To develop, maintain, and revise a model restraint and
26 seclusion plan for schools that includes the following elements:
27 (A) A statement on how students will be treated with dignity
28 and respect and how appropriate student behavior will be
29 promoted and taught.
30 (B) A statement ensuring that the school will use prevention,
31 positive behavior intervention and support, and conflict
32 de-escalation to eliminate or minimize the need for use of any
33 of the following:
34 (i) Seclusion.
35 (ii) Chemical restraint.
36 (iii) Mechanical restraint.
37 (iv) Physical restraint.
38 (v) Time-out.
39 (C) A statement ensuring that any behavioral intervention used
40 will be consistent with the student's most current behavioral
41 intervention plan, or individualized education program, if
42 applicable.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

213
1 (D) Definitions for restraint and seclusion, as defined in this
2 chapter.
3 (E) A statement ensuring that if a procedure listed in clause (B)
4 is used, the procedure will be used:
5 (i) as a last resort safety procedure, employed only after
6 another, less restrictive procedure has been implemented
7 without success; and
8 (ii) in a situation in which there is an imminent risk of injury
9 to the student, other students, school employees, or visitors to
10 the school.
11 (F) An indication that restraint or seclusion may be used only
12 for a short time period, or until the imminent risk of injury has
13 passed.
14 (G) A documentation and recording requirement governing
15 instances in which procedures listed in clause (B) are used,
16 including:
17 (i) how every incident will be documented and debriefed;
18 (ii) how responsibilities will be assigned to designated
19 employees for evaluation and oversight; and
20 (iii) designation of a school employee to be the keeper of
21 such documents.
22 (H) A requirement that the student's parent must be notified as
23 soon as possible when an incident involving the student occurs
24 that includes use of procedures listed in clause (B).
25 (I) A requirement that a copy of an incident report must be sent
26 to the student's parent after the student is subject to a procedure
27 listed in clause (B).
28 (J) Required recurrent training for appropriate school
29 employees on the appropriate use of effective alternatives to
30 physical restraint and seclusion, including the use of positive
31 behavioral intervention and support and conflict de-escalation.
32 The training must include the safe use of physical restraint and
33 seclusion in incidents involving imminent danger or serious
34 harm to the student, school employees, or others. Consideration
35 must be given to available school resources and the time
36 commitments of school employees.
37 (3) To accept and review reports from the public and make
38 nonbinding recommendations to the department of any suggested
39 action to be taken.

42 IC 20-35-3-1 regarding the:

214
1 (A) execution of the commission's duties under this section; and

26 commission council under subsection (b), the department shall provide
27 the commission council a response with regard to the commission's
28 council's recommendation in a manner prescribed by the department
29 within a reasonable time after the department receives the
30 recommendation from the commission. council.

20 SECTION 366. IC 21-18.5-1-7 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
21 CODE AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS
22 [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 7. All of the rights, duties,
23 obligations, and responsibilities transferred from the Indiana
24 commission on proprietary education to the board for proprietary
25 education established by IC 21-18.5-5-1 (before its expiration)
26 under this chapter are transferred to the commission for higher
27 education, including the following:
28 (1) The rights, liabilities, penalties, crimes, and proceedings
29 described in section 4(b) of this chapter continue and are
30 imposed and enforced by the commission for higher
31 education.
32 (2) The powers and duties described in section 5(c) of this
33 chapter are transferred to the commission for higher
34 education.
35 (3) An accreditation granted or a permit issued as described
36 in sections 5(g) of this chapter is treated as an authorization
37 by the commission for higher education.
38 (4) Any proceedings pending as described in section 5(i) of this

15 proprietary educational institution" means a degree granting and credit
16 bearing institution that provides instructional or educational services
17 or training, whether onsite, online, or through any combination of these
18 or other instructional modalities, and is accredited by an accrediting
19 agency recognized by the United States Department of Education or is
20 seeking and progressing toward accreditation by an accrediting agency
21 recognized by the United States Department of Education.

23 (1) An Indiana state educational institution or another Indiana

25 or in part by public funds.

27 or regulated by any other state regulatory board, agency, or
28 commission other than the board for proprietary education.
29 commission for higher education.
30 (3) An elementary or secondary school attended by students in
31 kindergarten or grades 1 through 12 and supported in whole or in
32 part by private tuition payments.

34 (A) is maintained or given by an employer or a group of

36 employer anticipates employing;

38 charge, for its members or apprentices;

35 five (5) years immediately preceding appointment; and

37 not representatives of the types of postsecondary credit bearing
38 proprietary educational institutions to be authorized.

38 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 6. (a) The career college student assurance
39 fund is established to provide indemnification to a student or an
40 enrollee of a postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
41 institution who suffers loss or damage as a result of:
42 (1) the failure or neglect of the postsecondary credit bearing
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

231
1 proprietary educational institution to faithfully perform all
2 agreements, express or otherwise, with the student, enrollee, one
3 (1) or both of the parents of the student or enrollee, or a guardian
4 of the student or enrollee as represented by the application for the
5 institution's authorization and the materials submitted in support
6 of that application;
7 (2) the failure or neglect of the postsecondary credit bearing
8 proprietary educational institution to maintain and operate a
9 course or courses of instruction or study in compliance with the
10 standards of this chapter; or
11 (3) an agent's misrepresentation in procuring the student's
12 enrollment.
13 (b) The board for proprietary education commission for higher
14 education shall administer the fund.
15 (c) The expenses of administering the fund shall be paid from
16 money in the fund.
17 (d) The treasurer of state shall invest the money in the fund not
18 currently needed to meet the obligations of the fund in the same
19 manner as other public funds may be invested.
20 (e) Money in the fund at the end of a state fiscal year does not revert
21 to the state general fund but remains available to be used for providing
22 money for reimbursements allowed under this chapter.
23 (f) Upon the fund acquiring fifty thousand dollars ($50,000), the
24 balance in the fund must not become less than fifty thousand dollars
25 ($50,000). If:
26 (1) a claim against the fund is filed that would, if paid in full,
27 require the balance of the fund to become less than fifty thousand
28 dollars ($50,000); and
29 (2) the board for proprietary education commission for higher
30 education determines that the student is eligible for a
31 reimbursement under the fund;
32 the board for proprietary education commission for higher education
33 shall prorate the amount of the reimbursement to ensure that the
34 balance of the fund does not become less than fifty thousand dollars
35 ($50,000), and the student is entitled to receive that balance of the
36 student's claim from the fund as money becomes available in the fund
37 from contributions to the fund required under this chapter.
38 (g) The board for proprietary education commission for higher
39 education shall ensure that all outstanding claim amounts described in
40 subsection (f) are paid as money in the fund becomes available in the
41 chronological order of the outstanding claims.
42 (h) A claim against the fund may not be construed to be a debt of the

232
1 state.
2 SECTION 381. IC 21-18.5-6-7, AS ADDED BY P.L.107-2012,
3 SECTION 58, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
4 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 7. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this
5 section, each postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
6 institution shall make quarterly contributions to the fund. The quarters
7 begin January 1, April 1, July 1, and October 1.
8 (b) For each quarter, each postsecondary credit bearing proprietary
9 educational institution shall make a contribution equal to the STEP
10 THREE amount derived under the following formula:
11 STEP ONE: Determine the total amount of tuition and fees earned
12 during the quarter.
13 STEP TWO: Multiply the STEP ONE amount by one-tenth of one
14 percent (0.1%).
15 STEP THREE: Add the STEP TWO amount and sixty dollars
16 ($60).
17 (c) After June 30, 2012, upon the career college student assurance
18 fund achieving at least an initial balance of five hundred thousand
19 dollars ($500,000), a postsecondary credit bearing proprietary
20 educational institution that contributes to the career college student
21 assurance fund when the initial quarterly contribution is required under
22 this chapter after the fund's establishment is not required to make
23 contributions to the fund.
24 (d) The board for proprietary education commission for higher
25 education shall determine the number of quarterly contributions
26 required for the career college student assurance fund to initially
27 accumulate five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000).
28 (e) Except as provided in subsections (a), (b), and (f), a
29 postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational institution that
30 begins making contributions to the career college student assurance
31 fund after the initial quarterly contribution as required under this
32 section shall make contributions to the fund for the same number of
33 quarters as determined by the board for proprietary education
34 commission for higher education under subsection (d).
35 (f) If, after a career college student assurance fund acquires five
36 hundred thousand dollars ($500,000), the balance in the fund becomes
37 less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars ($250,000), all
38 postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational institutions not
39 required to make contributions to the career college student assurance
40 fund as described in subsection (c) or (e) shall make contributions to
41 the career college student assurance fund for the number of quarters
42 necessary for the fund to accumulate five hundred thousand dollars

1 ($500,000).

1 that are appropriate to that type or class of postsecondary credit bearing

12 enrolled.

25 standards.

26 SECTION 441. IC 22-8-1.1-17.1 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
27 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 17.1. (a) Any
28 standard promulgated under this chapter shall prescribe the use of
29 labels or other appropriate forms of warning as are necessary to insure
30 that employees are apprised of all hazards to which they are exposed,
31 relevant symptoms and appropriate emergency treatment, and proper
32 conditions and precautions of safe use or exposure. Where appropriate,
33 such a standard shall also prescribe suitable protective equipment and
34 control or technological procedures to be used in connection with the
35 hazards and shall provide for monitoring or measuring employee
36 exposure at such locations and intervals and in such manner as may be
37 necessary for the protection of employees. In addition where
38 appropriate, any standard shall prescribe the type or frequency of
39 medical examinations or other tests which shall be made available by
40 the employer, at employer's cost, to employees exposed to hazards in
41 order to most effectively determine whether the health of the
42 employees is adversely affected by the exposure. Upon request, the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

259
1 results of examinations or tests shall be furnished to the department and
2 shall remain confidential within the department. At the request of the
3 employee, results shall be furnished to his physician.
4 (b) The commission, board, in promulgating standards dealing with
5 toxic materials or harmful physical agents, shall set the standard which
6 most adequately assures, to the extent feasible, on the basis of the best
7 available evidence, that no employee will suffer material impairment
8 of health or functional capacity even if the employee has regular
9 exposure to the hazard dealt with by the standard for the period of his
10 working life. Development of standards shall be based upon research,
11 demonstrations, experiments, and such other information as may be
12 appropriate. In addition to the attainment of the highest degree of
13 health and safety protection for the employee, other considerations
14 shall be the latest available scientific data in the field, the feasibility of
15 the standards, and experience gained under this and other health and
16 safety laws. Whenever practicable, the standard promulgated shall be
17 expressed in terms of objective criteria and of the performance desired.
18 (c) The commission, board, in promulgating standards, shall adopt
19 rules requiring employers to maintain accurate records of employee
20 exposures to potentially toxic material or harmful physical agents
21 which are required to be monitored or measured under the standards.
22 These rules shall provide employees or their representatives with an
23 opportunity to observe monitoring or measuring and to have access to
24 the records thereof. These rules shall also make appropriate provisions
25 for each employee to have access to such records as will indicate his
26 own exposure to toxic materials or harmful physical agents. Under
27 these rules, each employer shall notify any employee who is being
28 consistently exposed to toxic materials or harmful physical agents in
29 concentrations or at levels which exceed those prescribed by an
30 occupational safety and health standard and shall inform any employee
31 who is being thus exposed of the corrective action being taken.

40 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 19.1. Temporary
41 Variances. Any employer may apply to the commission board for a
42 temporary order granting a variance from a standard or any provision
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

260
1 thereof promulgated under this chapter. Such temporary order shall be
2 granted only if the employer establishes that he is unable to comply
3 with a standard by its effective date because of unavailability of
4 professional or technical personnel or of materials and equipment
5 needed to come into compliance with the standard or because necessary
6 construction or alteration of facilities cannot be completed by the
7 effective date; that he is taking all available steps to safeguard his
8 employees against the hazards covered by the standard; and that he has
9 an effective program for coming into compliance with a standard as
10 quickly as practicable. Any temporary order issued under this section
11 shall prescribe the practices, means, methods, operations and processes
12 which the employer must adopt and use while the order is in effect and
13 state in detail his program for coming into compliance with the
14 standard. Such a temporary order may be granted only after notice to
15 employees and an opportunity for a hearing. Said notice shall be given
16 to the authorized representative of the employees and be posted at or
17 near the location for which the variance is sought. No order for a
18 temporary variance may be in effect for longer than the period needed
19 by the employer to achieve compliance with the standard or one (1)
20 year, whichever is shorter, except that such an order may be renewed
21 not more than twice, so long as the requirements of this paragraph are
22 met and if an application for renewal is filed at least ninety (90) days
23 prior to the expiration date of the order.

30 (1) the person;
31 (2) a person residing in or intending to reside in the dwelling after

34 (c) For purposes of this section only, discrimination includes the
35 following:
36 (1) A refusal to permit, at the expense of the person with a
37 disability, reasonable modifications of existing premises occupied
38 or to be occupied by the person if the modifications may be

42 necessary to afford the person equal opportunity to use and enjoy
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

263
1 a dwelling.
2 (3) In connection with the design and construction of covered
3 multifamily dwellings for first occupancy after March 13, 1991,
4 a failure to design and construct those dwellings in a manner that:
5 (A) the public use and common use parts of the dwellings are
6 readily accessible to and usable by persons with disabilities;
7 (B) all the doors are designed to allow passage into and within
8 all premises within the dwellings and are sufficiently wide to
9 allow passage by persons with disabilities in wheelchairs; and
10 (C) all premises within the dwellings contain the following
11 features of adaptive design:
12 (i) An accessible route into and through the dwelling.
13 (ii) Light switches, electrical outlets, thermostats, and other
14 environmental controls in accessible locations.
15 (iii) Reinforcements in bathroom walls to allow later
16 installation of grab bars.
17 (iv) Usable kitchens and bathrooms so that an individual in
18 a wheelchair can maneuver about the space.
19 (d) As used in subsection (c), "covered multifamily dwellings"
20 means:
21 (1) buildings consisting of four (4) or more units if the buildings
22 have one (1) or more elevators; and
23 (2) ground floor units in other buildings consisting of four (4) or
24 more units.
25 (e) Compliance with the rules of the fire prevention and building
26 safety commission department of homeland security that incorporate
27 by reference the appropriate requirements of the American National
28 Standard for buildings and facilities providing accessibility and
29 usability for people with physical disabilities (ANSI A117.1) satisfies
30 the requirements of subsection (c)(3)(C).
31 (f) This section does not require that a dwelling be made available
32 to an individual whose tenancy would constitute a direct threat to the
33 health or safety of other individuals or whose tenancy would result in

11 "Active workings" means all places in a mine that are ventilated and

15 connection with examinations to ensure that the belt, belt drives, dump
16 points, air movement, roof, and ribs of a mine are in safe condition.
17 "Board" refers to the mining board established under
18 IC 22-10-1.5-2.

20 is produced for sale, exchange, or commercial use.
21 "Director" means the director of the bureau of mines and mine
22 safety established under IC 22-1-1-4.
23 "Hoisting engineer" means an individual who is capable of
24 transporting people and material in and out of a mine by means of a
25 hoist.

27 by the operator, state and federal coal mine inspectors, and, to the
28 extent required by law, any other person.
29 "Mine" means an underground commercial coal mine.
30 "Mine electrician" means a properly certified individual who can
31 perform electrical work in:
32 (1) a surface coal mine;
33 (2) surface areas of underground coal mines; and
34 (3) underground coal mines.
35 "Mine examiner" means a properly certified person designated by
36 the mine foreman to examine the mine for gas and other dangers. A
37 mine examiner may temporarily act as a section foreman if designated
38 to act as such by the mine foreman.
39 "Mine foreman" means the person charged with the responsibility
40 of the general supervision of the underground working of a mine and
41 the persons employed in the mine and for the health and safety of those
42 employees.

35 upon examination, prove themselves competent and qualified.

37 has been held in the presence of a member of the board and signed by
38 the chairman of the board. an employee of the bureau. A certificate
39 of competency may not be issued to any person whose grade is less
40 than seventy-five percent (75%) for any certification other than that of
41 a certificate for mine electrician, which requires a passing grade of not
42 less than eighty percent (80%). The board bureau shall observe the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

270
1 requirements set forth in this section in conducting the examinations.
2 (b) (c) An applicant for a mine foreman certificate must have at
3 least four (4) years of experience underground in coal mines. However,
4 a person who has graduated and holds a degree in engineering or an
5 approved four (4) year program in coal mining technology from an
6 accredited school, college, or university is required to have only two (2)
7 years of practical underground mining experience to qualify for the
8 examination. A person who has graduated and holds a two (2) year
9 associate in applied science degree in coal mining technology from an
10 accredited school, college, or university is required to have only three
11 (3) years of practical underground mining experience to qualify for the
12 examination. An applicant must prove to the board bureau by written
13 and oral examination and by demonstration, where applicable, that the
14 applicant has a thorough knowledge of:
15 (1) the theory and practice of coal mining;
16 (2) the nature and properties of poisonous, noxious, and explosive
17 gases and methods for their detection and control;
18 (3) the requirements of the coal mining laws of this state; and
19 (4) the responsibilities and duties of a mine foreman under such
20 laws;
21 and that the applicant is otherwise qualified by law.
22 (c) (d) An applicant for a mine examiner certificate must have at
23 least three (3) years of experience underground in coal mines.
24 However, a person who has graduated and holds a degree in
25 engineering or an associate in applied science degree in coal mining
26 technology from an accredited school, college, or university is required
27 to have only two (2) years of practical underground mining experience
28 to qualify for the examination. An applicant must prove to the board
29 bureau by written and oral examination and by demonstration, where
30 applicable, that the applicant has a thorough knowledge of:
31 (1) the nature and properties of poisonous, noxious, and explosive
32 gases and methods for their detection and control;
33 (2) the practical aspects of coal mining pertaining especially to
34 ventilation and roof control; and
35 (3) the responsibilities of a mine examiner under coal mining laws
36 of this state;
37 and that the applicant is otherwise qualified by law.
38 (d) (e) An applicant for a shot-firer certificate must have at least one
39 (1) year of underground experience and must have been properly
40 trained in a course approved by the director in the safe use and
41 handling of explosives. An applicant must prove to the board bureau
42 by written and oral examination and by demonstration, where
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

271
1 applicable, that the applicant has a working knowledge of:
2 (1) the proper handling and use of explosives and blasting devices
3 and the danger connected therewith;
4 (2) the nature and properties of poisonous, noxious, and explosive
5 gases and methods for their detection;
6 (3) the coal mining laws of the state pertaining to ventilation, roof
7 control, and blasting; and
8 (4) the responsibilities of a shot-firer under applicable mining
9 laws;

17 (3) has at least one (1) year mining experience;
18 (4) has at least twenty (20) hours practical experience under the
19 supervision of a certified hoisting engineer; and
20 (5) is otherwise qualified by law.
21 (f) (g) An applicant for a belt examiner certificate must have at least

1 (5) 30 CFR 75 subparts F-K; and

1 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 11.1. (a) As used

4 (b) A practitioner shall conduct his duties as he is so certified in
5 accordance with the standards established by the board bureau under
6 section 9(a) section 9 of this chapter and is subject to the exercise of
7 the disciplinary sanctions under subsection (e), if after a hearing, the
8 board finds:
9 (1) the practitioner has employed or knowingly cooperated in
10 fraud or material deception in order to obtain a certificate, or has
11 engaged in fraud or material deception in the course of
12 professional services or activities, or has advertised services in a
13 false or misleading manner;
14 (2) the practitioner has been convicted of a crime which has a
15 direct bearing on the practitioner's ability to continue to practice
16 competently;
17 (3) a practitioner has knowingly violated section 12 of this
18 chapter, or any rule adopted by the board under section 9(A)
19 under this chapter; bureau under section 9 of this chapter;
20 (4) a practitioner has continued to practice as certified although
21 he has become unfit to practice due to:
22 (A) professional incompetence;
23 (B) failure to keep abreast of current professional theory or
24 practice;
25 (C) physical or mental disability; or
26 (D) addiction or severe dependency upon alcohol or other drugs
27 which endangers the public by impairing a practitioner's ability
28 to practice safely;
29 (5) a practitioner has engaged in a course of lewd or immoral
30 conduct in connection with the delivery of services to clients; or
31 (6) a practitioner has allowed his name or certificate issued to him
32 under this chapter to be used in connection with any individual
33 who renders mining services beyond the scope of his training,
34 experience or competence.
35 (c) The board bureau may order a practitioner to submit to a
36 reasonable physical or mental examination if his physical or mental
37 capacity to practice safely is at issue in a disciplinary proceeding.
38 (d) Failure to comply with a board bureau order to submit to a
39 physical or mental examination shall render a practitioner liable to the
40 summary revocation procedures under subsection (f).
41 (e) The board bureau may impose any of the following sanctions,
42 singly or in combination, when it finds that a practitioner is guilty of
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

274
1 any offense under subsection (b):
2 (1) permanently revoke a practitioner's certificate;
3 (2) suspend a practitioner's certificate;
4 (3) censure a practitioner;
5 (4) issue a letter of reprimand; or
6 (5) place a practitioner on probation status and require the
7 practitioner to:
8 (A) report regularly to the board bureau upon the matters
9 which are the basis of probation;
10 (B) limit practice to those areas prescribed by the board;
11 bureau; or
12 (C) continue or renew professional education under a
13 practitioner approved by the board bureau until satisfactory
14 degree of skill has been attained in those areas which are the
15 basis of the probation.
16 The board bureau may withdraw the probation if it finds that the
17 deficiency which required disciplinary action has been remedied.
18 (f) The board bureau may summarily suspend a practitioner's
19 certificate for a period of ninety (90) days in advance of a final
20 adjudication or during the appeals process if the board bureau finds
21 that a practitioner represents a clear and immediate danger to the public
22 health and safety if he is allowed to continue to practice. The summary
23 suspension may be renewed upon a hearing before the board, bureau,
24 and each renewal may be for a period of ninety (90) days or less.
25 (g) A certificate issued under this chapter is automatically
26 suspended upon the conviction of the practitioner of a felony under 30
27 U.S.C. 820. A practitioner whose certificate is suspended under this
28 subsection may apply for reinstatement under subsection (h) if at least
29 five (5) years have elapsed from the practitioner's date of discharge
30 from probation, imprisonment, or parole from the felony.
31 (h) The board bureau may reinstate a certificate which has been
32 suspended under this chapter if, after a hearing, the board bureau is
33 satisfied that the applicant is able to practice with reasonable skill and
34 safety. As a condition for reinstatement, the board bureau may impose
35 disciplinary or corrective measures authorized under this chapter.
36 (i) The board bureau shall seek to achieve consistency in the
37 application of the sanctions authorized in this section, and significant
38 departures from prior decisions involving similar conduct shall be
39 explained in the board's findings or orders.

4 subject to subsection (f).
5 (c) The director's record shall include the names of applicants for
6 certificates and the names of persons to whom certificates are issued,
7 correlated with the certificate numbers.
8 (d) An applicant for a certificate must pay the director at the time of
9 application a fee of five dollars ($5). All money received under this
10 subsection shall be deposited in the mine safety fund established by
11 IC 22-10-12-16. The board bureau may set a different fee by rule
12 under IC 22-10-1.5-4.
13 (e) The board director of the bureau shall report to the director the
14 record the names of all persons issued certificates, the amount of
15 money received, the names of all persons refused certificates, and the
16 reasons for the refusals, and such reports shall be open for inspection
17 by interested persons.
18 (f) If a person has been convicted of a felony under 30 U.S.C. 820
19 and fewer than five (5) years have elapsed from the person's date of
20 discharge from probation, imprisonment, or parole, the person may not:
21 (1) obtain a certificate of competency;
22 (2) be employed to work with a person who does possess a
23 certificate; or
24 (3) obtain a permit to work toward a certificate of competency;
25 under subsection (a).

1 consistent with Mine Safety Administration requirements.

4 SECTION 66, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
5 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 2. (a) Except as provided in subsections (b) and
6 (d) and section 2.5 of this chapter, an owner of a public building shall
7 not permit an exit to be locked or obstructed in any manner that denies
8 the public a continuous and unobstructed means of egress while
9 lawfully occupied by anyone who is not an officer or an employee.
10 (b) The commission department may adopt rules under IC 4-22-2
11 that:
12 (1) allow the owner of a public building to equip an exit with a
13 special egress control device;
14 (2) limit the circumstances under which a special egress control
15 device may be used; and
16 (3) allow an exit that was in compliance with the rules of the
17 commission department when the exit was constructed to be
18 equipped with a special egress control device.
19 (c) An owner of a public building shall not permit a fire alarm to be
20 disconnected or otherwise rendered inoperative, except in cases of
21 routine maintenance or for repair.
22 (d) A school that has one (1) or more employees shall develop a
23 plan to address unplanned fire alarm activation as part of its emergency
24 operations plan.
25 (e) A school's emergency operations plan for unplanned fire alarm
26 activation shall include procedures for the following:
27 (1) Evacuation of the building when the fire alarm is heard. A
28 school with a fire alarm panel that allows for a positive fire alarm
29 sequence may:
30 (A) develop a plan to investigate an unplanned fire alarm
31 activation before activating the audible and visual alarms
32 requiring evacuation;
33 (B) designate school officials to acknowledge that an alarm has
34 been activated and initiate an investigation within fifteen (15)
35 seconds;
36 (C) secure-in-place for up to three (3) minutes in order for a
37 designated school official to determine, by investigation, if an
38 active shooter is on the property; and
39 (D) following the three (3) minute period under clause (C), the
40 school must evacuate, unless an active shooter has been
41 verified to be on the school's property.
42 (2) Compliance with all provisions of 675 IAC 28-1-28.

20 SECTION 482. IC 22-12-2.1 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
21 CODE AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS
22 [EFFECTIVE UPON PASSAGE]:
23 Chapter 2.1. Transition of Responsibilities for Fire Prevention
24 and Building Safety
25 Sec. 1. (a) On July 1, 2026, all powers, duties, agreements, and
26 liabilities of the fire prevention and building safety commission are
27 transferred to the department.
28 (b) On July 1, 2026, all records and property of the fire
29 prevention and building safety commission, including
30 appropriations and other funds under the control or supervision of
31 the fire prevention and building safety commission, are transferred
32 to the department.
33 (c) After June 30, 2026, any amounts owed to the fire prevention
34 and building safety commission before July 1, 2026, are considered
35 to be owed to the department as the successor agency.
36 (d) After June 30, 2026, a reference to the fire prevention and
37 building safety commission in a statute, rule, or other document is
38 considered a reference to the department.
39 (e) Proceedings pending before the fire prevention and building
40 safety commission on July 1, 2026, shall be transferred from the
41 fire prevention and building safety commission to the department
42 and treated as if initiated by the department.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

287
1 (f) A license or permit issued by the fire prevention and building
2 safety commission before July 1, 2026, shall be treated after June
3 30, 2026, as a certification issued by the department.
4 (g) The rules adopted by the fire prevention and building safety
5 commission before July 1, 2026:
6 (1) are considered, after June 30, 2026, rules of the
7 department; and
8 (2) remain in place without expiration until the rules are
9 codified by the general assembly.
10 (h) Not later than July 1, 2027, and each year thereafter, the
11 department shall submit a report to the legislative council in an
12 electronic format under IC 5-14-6 making recommendations for
13 codification and amendment of the rules of the fire prevention and
14 building safety commission. The legislative council shall assign the
15 report to be reviewed by an study committee established under
16 IC 2-5-1.3. In preparing the report required by this subsection, the
17 department shall consult with industry experts, professionals, and
18 interested stakeholders who have indicated an interest in the
19 subject matter of the recommendations contained in the report.

30 Pressure Vessel Code.
31 (2) The National Board of Boiler and Pressure Vessel Inspectors
32 Inspection Code.
33 (3) The American Petroleum Institute 510 Pressure Vessel
34 Inspection Code.
35 (4) Any subsequent editions of the codes listed in subdivisions (1)
36 through (3).
37 (d) The commission department shall adopt rules under IC 4-22-2
38 to create equipment laws applicable to regulated amusement devices.

25 variance under this section only if the department places the
26 application for the variance on the commission's agenda.
27 (b) To qualify for a variance, an applicant must pay the fee set under
28 IC 22-12-6-6 and file an application, on a form approved by the
29 department, that contains facts demonstrating that:
30 (1) compliance with the rule will impose an undue hardship upon
31 the applicant or prevent the preservation of an architecturally

33 structure; and
34 (2) either:
35 (A) noncompliance with the rule; or
36 (B) compliance with an alternative requirement approved by the
37 body considering the variance application;
38 will not be adverse to the public health, safety, or welfare.

1 or welfare of the public;

14 not take effect sooner than one hundred eighty (180) days after the

16 elimination of an authorization is subject to IC 4-21.5.

18 voluntary. However, if a person elects to comply with an
19 authorization, the failure to comply with any provision of the
20 authorization is a violation of the building, fire safety, or
21 equipment laws of Indiana.

1 marshal of eight hundred dollars ($800). The state fire marshal may
2 adjust the fee every three (3) years to ensure that the fee defrays the

5 prevention and building safety commission. department. However, the
6 fee for each brand family may not exceed one thousand dollars

8 (g) If a manufacturer has certified a cigarette under this section, and
9 after submitting the certification, makes a change to the cigarette that
10 is likely to alter the cigarette's compliance with the reduced cigarette
11 ignition propensity standards required by this chapter, that cigarette
12 may not be sold or offered for sale in Indiana until the manufacturer
13 retests the cigarette under the testing standards in section 13 or 15 of
14 this chapter and maintains records of that retesting as required by
15 section 16 of this chapter. An altered cigarette that does not meet the
16 performance standard in section 13 or 15 of this chapter may not be
17 sold in Indiana.

30 SECTION 554. IC 22-15-3.5 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
31 CODE AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS
32 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]:
33 Chapter 3.5. Self Certification by Design Professional
34 Sec. 1. The definitions in IC 22-15-3.2 apply throughout this
35 chapter.
36 Sec. 2. (a) Notwithstanding any other law, an applicant for a
37 construction project requiring a design release for a Class 1
38 structure under IC 22-12-3 may satisfy the design release
39 requirement through self-certification.
40 (b) Self-certification may be used only for projects that are
41 required to be signed and sealed by an Indiana registered design
42 professional. The department may exclude projects from
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

320
1 self-certification that involve high-hazard occupancies, essential
2 facilities, or other structures determined to pose an elevated risk
3 to life safety.
4 Sec. 3. To self-certify a project, an applicant for a design release
5 must:
6 (1) be an Indiana registered design professional in good
7 standing with the applicable registration board;
8 (2) be a registered design professional for at least two (2)
9 years;
10 (3) have completed at least five (5) projects in Indiana as the
11 registered design professional of record or as the registered
12 design professional responsible for the production of the
13 construction documents; and
14 (4) maintain professional liability insurance of at least five
15 hundred thousand dollars ($500,000) per claim and one
16 million dollars ($1,000,000) aggregate.
17 Sec. 4. The department shall develop an electronic application
18 form for self-certifying applicants.
19 Sec. 5. The certifying design professional shall:
20 (1) perform a comprehensive review of the construction
21 documents; and
22 (2) submit to the department a signed certification, under
23 penalty of perjury, affirming that the plans comply with all
24 applicable state building codes and rules.
25 Sec. 6. Upon receipt of a complete certification and required
26 documentation, the department shall, not later than the next
27 business day, provide to the applicant:
28 (1) a copy of the design release; or
29 (2) a confirmation number that serves as a temporary design
30 release until the applicant receives a copy of the design
31 release;
32 without conducting a technical plan review.
33 Sec. 7. The department may:
34 (1) audit self-certified projects;
35 (2) require corrective action for non-compliance;
36 (3) revoke or suspend a professional's authority to provide
37 certifications; and
38 (4) refer violations to the appropriate licensing board or
39 impose civil penalties as authorized by law.
40 Sec. 8. The department may collect plan review fees for projects
41 that are released without a plan review.
42 Sec. 9. Nothing in this section limits the authority of state or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

321
1 local inspection officials to conduct inspections or enforce
2 compliance during construction.
3 Sec. 10. An applicant who is qualified to self-certify may apply
4 for a design release under:
5 (1) this chapter; or
6 (2) IC 22-15-3.
7 Sec. 11. Participation in the self-certification program is
8 voluntary. Nothing in this section prohibits the department from
9 reviewing or not reviewing an application in accordance with
10 IC 22-15-3.

23 (2) when the regulated lifting device is altered.
24 (c) After a regulated lifting device has been installed or altered, an
25 applicant shall apply for an initial operating certificate. The department
26 shall issue an initial operating certificate for a regulated lifting device
27 if:
28 (1) the applicant demonstrates:
29 (A) through an acceptance inspection made by an elevator
30 inspector licensed under section 11 of this chapter that the
31 regulated lifting device covered by the application complies
32 with the laws governing its construction, repair, maintenance,
33 and operation; and
34 (B) that the applicant has paid the fee set under
35 IC 22-12-6-6(a)(7); and
36 (2) the department verifies, through an inspection, that the
37 regulated lifting device complies with the laws governing the
38 construction, repair, maintenance, and operation of the regulated
39 lifting device.
40 (d) The department shall issue a renewal operating certificate if the
41 applicant:
42 (1) demonstrates through the completion of applicable safety tests
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

325
1 that the regulated lifting device complies with the laws governing
2 the construction, repair, maintenance, and operation of the
3 regulated lifting device;
4 (2) submits results of all applicable safety tests, including failed
5 safety tests for the regulated lifting device; and
6 (3) has paid the fee set under IC 22-12-6-6(a)(7).
7 (e) The department may issue a temporary operating permit to an
8 applicant under this section who does not comply with subsection
9 (c)(1)(A) for a new or altered regulated lifting device or subsection
10 (d)(1) for an existing unaltered regulated lifting device. The applicant
11 must pay the fee set under IC 22-12-6-6(a)(7) to qualify for the
12 temporary operating permit. Except as provided in subsection (f), the
13 permit, including all renewal periods, is limited to sixty (60) days.
14 (f) The department may renew a temporary operating permit issued
15 under subsection (e) for thirty (30) day periods during the construction
16 of a building if the regulated lifting device is used for the transportation
17 of construction personnel, tools, and materials.
18 (g) The responsibilities of the department under this section may be
19 carried out by a political subdivision that is approved by the
20 commission department under IC 22-13-2-10.
21 (h) A copy of the operating certificate shall be displayed in or on
22 each regulated lifting device or in an associated machine room. In
23 addition to the requirements of this subsection, the two-dimensional bar
24 code assigned to an elevator shall be displayed in or on each elevator
25 in a location that is easily viewed and scanned by a person riding on the
26 elevator.
27 (i) A licensed elevator mechanic shall perform the maintenance on
28 a regulated lifting device.

30 SECTION 309, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
31 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 6. (a) The following definitions
32 apply to sections 7 through 16 of this chapter:
33 (1) "Competency examination" means an examination that
34 thoroughly tests the scope of the knowledge and skill of the
35 applicant for the license.
36 (2) "Elevator apprentice" means an individual who works under
37 the direct supervision of a licensed elevator mechanic. The term
38 includes an individual commonly known as an elevator helper
39 while working under the direct supervision of a licensed elevator
40 mechanic.
41 (3) "Elevator contractor" means a person who alone or with other
42 persons, constructs, repairs, alters, remodels, adds to, subtracts
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

326
1 from, or improves a regulated lifting device and who is
2 responsible for substantially all the regulated lifting devices
3 within the entire project, or who fabricates elevator lifting devices
4 substantially completed and ready for installation.
5 (4) "Elevator inspector" means an individual who conducts the
6 acceptance inspection of a regulated lifting device required by
7 section 4(c)(1)(A) of this chapter.
8 (5) "Elevator mechanic" means an individual who engages in the
9 construction, reconstruction, alteration, maintenance, mechanical,
10 or electrical work or adjustments of a regulated lifting device.
11 (6) "License" means a certificate issued by the department that
12 confers upon the holder the privilege to act as an elevator
13 contractor, elevator inspector, or elevator mechanic.
14 (7) "Licensing program" means the program for licensing elevator
15 contractors, elevator inspectors, and elevator mechanics
16 established under this section and sections 7 through 16 of this
17 chapter.
18 (8) "Municipality" has the meaning set forth in IC 36-1-2-11.
19 (9) "Person" means:
20 (A) a natural person;
21 (B) the partners or members of a partnership or a limited
22 partnership;
23 (C) a state educational institution; or
24 (D) a corporation or the officers, directors, and employees of
25 the corporation.
26 (10) "Practitioner" means a person that holds:
27 (A) an unlimited license;
28 (B) a limited or probationary license;
29 (C) a temporary license;
30 (D) an emergency license; or
31 (E) an inactive license.
32 (b) The commission and the department shall establish a program
33 to license elevator contractors, elevator mechanics, and elevator
34 inspectors.
35 (c) The department shall issue a license as an elevator contractor, an
36 elevator mechanic, or an elevator inspector to a person who qualifies
37 and complies with the provisions of the licensing program. A person
38 who receives a license under this chapter is subject to the supervision
39 and control of the department.
40 (d) The department may contract with public and private
41 institutions, agencies, businesses, and organizations to implement all
42 or part of its duties established under this chapter.

16 of instruction that must be attended and completed within one (1) year
17 before a license renewal.

19 continuing education of an individual holding a license regarding new
20 and existing provisions of the rules of the commission department
21 may include:
22 (1) programs sponsored by the commission; department;
23 (2) trade association seminars;
24 (3) labor training programs; or
25 (4) joint labor management apprenticeship and journeyman
26 upgrade training programs.
27 For an individual's completion of a continuing education course to
28 satisfy the individual's continuing education requirement under this
29 chapter, the continuing education provider, instructor and the
30 curriculum must have been approved by the department.
31 (e) All instructors of continuing education courses must be approved
32 by the department. If an instructor is approved by the department, has
33 worked as an instructor teaching a curriculum approved by the
34 department at any time within the year preceding the expiration date of
35 the license, and submits proof of this work to the department, the
36 instructor is exempt from the requirements of subsection (c).

39 (10) years on forms designed and distributed by the department.

41 education required under this chapter before the expiration of the
42 individual's license due to temporary physical or mental disability may
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

332
1 apply for a waiver from the department in accordance with the
2 following:
3 (1) A waiver application must be submitted to the department on
4 a form established by the department.
5 (2) A waiver application must be signed and accompanied by an
6 affidavit signed by the physician of the applicant attesting to the
7 applicant's temporary disability.
8 (h) After the cessation of the temporary disability, the applicant
9 must submit to the department a certification from the same physician,
10 if the physician is still the treating physician of the applicant, or from
11 a subsequent treating physician attesting to the termination of the
12 temporary disability.
13 (i) Upon the submission of the certification under subsection (h), the
14 department shall issue a temporary waiver of the continuing education
15 requirement. A temporary waiver is valid for ninety (90) days after the
16 date of issue and allows the individual to work as an elevator
17 contractor, elevator inspector, or elevator mechanic without the
18 completion of the continuing education requirement for ninety (90)
19 days.
20 (j) A temporary waiver of the continuing education requirement may
21 not be renewed.

18 (1) Apply for an operating permit on a form approved by the
19 department.
20 (2) Demonstrate through an inspection, performed by an inspector
21 licensed under section 5 of this chapter, that the regulated boiler
22 or pressure vessel covered by the application complies with the
23 rules adopted by the commission. department.
24 (3) Submit a report of the inspection conducted under subdivision
25 (2) to the department.
26 (4) Pay the fee set under IC 22-12-6-6(a)(8).
27 (e) The commission department may, by rule adopted under
28 IC 4-22-2, specify:
29 (1) a period between inspections of more than one (1) year; and
30 (2) an expiration date for an operating permit longer than one (1)
31 year from the date of issuance.
32 However, the commission department may not set an inspection
33 period of greater than five (5) years or issue an operating permit valid
34 for a period of more than five (5) years for regulated pressure vessels
35 or steam generating equipment that is an integral part of a continuous
36 processing unit.
37 (f) For any inspection conducted by the department under this
38 section, the department may designate an inspector licensed under
39 section 5 of this chapter to act as the department's agent for purposes
40 of the inspection.
41 (g) The commission department may adopt rules under IC 4-22-2
42 to implement this chapter.

35 forms and evidence.
36 This subsection does not require the agency to issue a renewal license

38 (g) The agency may delay issuing a license renewal for up to one
39 hundred twenty (120) days after the renewal date for the purpose of
40 permitting the board to investigate information received by the agency
41 that the applicant for renewal may have committed an act for which the
42 applicant may be disciplined. If the agency delays issuing a license
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

348
1 renewal, the agency shall notify the applicant that the applicant is being
2 investigated. Except as provided in subsection (h), before the end of the
3 one hundred twenty (120) day period, the board shall do one (1) of the
4 following:
5 (1) Deny the license renewal following a personal appearance by
6 the applicant before the board.
7 (2) Issue the license renewal upon satisfaction of all other
8 conditions for renewal.
9 (3) Issue the license renewal and file a complaint under IC 25-1-7.
10 (4) Upon agreement of the applicant and the board and following
11 a personal appearance by the applicant before the board, renew
12 the license and place the applicant on probation status under
13 IC 25-1-9-9.
14 (h) If an individual fails to appear before the board under subsection
15 (g), the board may take action on the applicant's license allowed under
16 subsection (g)(1), (g)(2), or (g)(3).
17 (i) The applicant's license remains valid until the final determination
18 of the board is rendered unless the renewal is denied or the license is
19 summarily suspended under IC 25-1-9-10.
20 (j) The license of the applicant for a license renewal remains valid
21 during the one hundred twenty (120) day period unless the license
22 renewal is denied following a personal appearance by the applicant
23 before the board before the end of the one hundred twenty (120) day
24 period. If the one hundred twenty (120) day period expires without
25 action by the board, the license shall be automatically renewed at the
26 end of the one hundred twenty (120) day period.
27 (k) The board's renewal of a license does not preclude the board
28 from imposing sanctions on the licensee as a result of a complaint filed
29 by the attorney general after renewal of the license.
30 (l) Notwithstanding any other statute, the agency may stagger
31 license or certificate renewal cycles. However, if a renewal cycle for a
32 specific board or committee is changed, the agency must obtain the
33 approval of the affected board or committee.
34 (m) An application for a license, certificate, registration, or permit
35 is abandoned without an action of the board, if the applicant does not
36 complete the requirements to complete the application within one (1)
37 year after the date on which the application was filed. However, the
38 board may, for good cause shown, extend the validity of the application
39 for additional thirty (30) day periods. An application submitted after
40 the abandonment of an application is considered a new application.
